text: CERESOLE, or CERISOLE, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 13 m. WNW of Alba, and 4 m. E of Carmagnole. Pop. 1,800. It is noted as the scene of a victory gained by the French over the Imperialists in 1544.

CERESTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Alpes, cant. of Reillanne, 13 m. WNW of Forcalquier. Pop. 1,141. It is supposed to have been the Catinaca of the territory of Gallia Narbonensis; and it contains some Roman antiquities.
CERESTE, or CEYRESTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of La Ciotat, 15 m. ESE of Marseilles. Pop. 700. It is of great antiquity, and was formerly fortified.
CERES-TOWN, a village of Keating township, M'Kean co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 198 m. NW of Harrisburg, on a branch of the Alleghany.

CERET, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Pyrenees- Orientales. The arrond. comprises an area of 92,400 hect., and contains 4 cant.: viz., Argeles, Arles-sur- Tech, Ceret, and Prats-de-Mollo. Pop. in 1831, 35,421; in 1841, 40,192; and in 1846, 41,700.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,135; in 1841, 10,084.—The town is 19 m. SSW of Perpignan, and 5 m. from the Spanish frontier. It is situated at the foot of the Pyrenees, 97 metres, or 318 ft. above sea-level, and near the r. bank of the Tech, which is here crossed by a lofty single-arched bridge. Pop. in 1789, 2,200; in 1836, 3,302; in 1841, 3,325; and in 1846, 3,510. It contains a fine town-house, and a communal college; and has some manufactories of leather and plate-iron. Oil, colonial grain, and leather, form the chief articles of its local trade. A fair is held once a-year. CEREZO-DE-ABAXO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 32 m. ENE of Segovia, partido and 11 m. ESE of Sepulveda.
CEREZO-DE-ARRIBA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 33 m. NE of Segovia, partido and 10 m. E of Sepulveda.

CERFFONTAINE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, 6 m. WSW of Phillipeville, on the r. bank of the Heure. Pop. 1,000. It contains manufactories of lace, a cannon foundry, and some ironworks. In 1793, the Austrians were here defeated by the French.
CERGUE (SAINT), or SANFREGO, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 6 m. NW of Nyon, at the foot of Mont Dole. Pop. 219.
CERGY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Pontaise. Pop. 1,016.
CERIANA, a village of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 4 m. N of San Remo, and 26 m. ENE of Nice. Pop. 2,000.
CERIBAN, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Rumelia, sanjak of Gallipoli, on the S side of the gulf and 10 m. E of Enos.
CERIGNES. See CERINA.

CERIGNOLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, district and 26 m. SE of Foggia. Pop. 10,350. It contains a college, several churches, and an hospital; has some linen manufactories, and an annual fair .- An engagement between the French and Spaniards, in which the Duke de Nemours lost his life, took place in the vicinity in 1503. The surrounding territory is fertile.

CERIGO, the most southerly of the Ionian islands, in N lat. 36° 6', E long. 22° 50'. It lies at a considerable distance from the others, between the S extremity of the Morea and the island of Candia, about 12 m. from the former, and 45 m. from the latter. Its form is triangular, with its base towards the S. Its extreme length from NNW to SSE is 19 m.; breadth 12 m.; circumf. about 50 m. It contains a pop. of between 8,000 and 9,000, mostly composed of refugees from the Morea, and including the little island of Cerigotto, which lies half-way between it and Candia, it has an area of 116 sq. m. It is exceedingly rugged and mountainous. Its W and S shores are rocky, abrupt, and precipitous; the E are flat and shelving; and the surface of limestone rock, broken into numerous chasms and ravines, presents in many places bold and picturesque scenery, intersected by a few small valleys of considerable beauty and fertility. The mountains generally run NW and SE. The southernmost chain intersects the SW of the island. Its highest summit, the Santa Elessa, does not exceed 1,400 ft. The chain which forms the high land in the centre of the island is called Lachnos. The third chain runs along the E side of the island. Except at the N end of the island, the mountains generally have round-backed and table-shaped forms. Two principal valleys of moderate breadth separate the three chains. In these valleys the bounding mountains have a gradual ascent; but in the lateral valleys they often present immense mural precipices. No rivers of any importance occur. Nearly the whole of the N part of the island consists of primitive slaty rocks; the higher parts of the central and S districts are chiefly transition rock. Most parts of the island which admit of it are under cultivation; but deficiency of vegetation is a conspicuous character throughout; and over a great part nothing is visible but the bare rock. There are scarcely any trees except olives, and these are few and stunted in their growth, owing to the tempestuous blasts to which ዸ፟ቜኇቜ፞፞ጜ፝ዸ፟ቜቜ፟ቔ፟ቔ፟፟፟፟፟ቔ፟፟፟ቜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ጜ፟ CER the island is exposed during the winter-season. C. produces in small quantity corn, oil, wine, raisins, honey, wax, cheese, cotton. The average number of acres cultivated is about 12,000; of pasture-land, 5,000; uncultivated, 66,000. Wheat is sown in autumn, and reaped in June. Maize is sown in May or June, and is ripe in September or October. The quantity of oil made is about 1,438 barrels. Sufficient cotton and flax are grown for home-consumption. Besides olives, the vegetation of C. presents lemons, oranges, walnuts, almonds, figs, cypresses, pomegranates, and palms. The brushwood consists almost entirely of mastic and myrtles. The elevation of C. secures the advantage of a constant breeze, with a reduced temp.; and though much exposed to storms and gales, the extremes of heat and cold are less strikingly experienced than in the more northern islands. There are no marshes; the soil is light and porous; there are no rivers or permanent streams; and the rain speedily finds its way to the sea through the deep ravines and gullies with which the island is intersected. The principal military station is a fort on the SE side of the island, on the summit of a rock rising abruptly from the sea to the height of 500 ft., and insulated, except on the N side, where it slopes and communicates with the ridge of land whereon the principal town is built. Both the town and fort are partly encircled by a chain of bare ragged mountains, intersected by two deep ravines or watercourses, through which the rain from the high grounds finds its way to the ocean.—The island is divided into the 5 districts of Citta, Levadi, Melapotamo, Castrisso, and Potamo.—The principal town or port of C. is Kapsali, at the SW angle; the only other port is San Nicolo, at the SE angle. The water is deep enough in the former port for ships of any burden; but it is much exposed to the SW and SE gales. Port San Nicolo has good anchorage-ground, and is well sheltered.—This island was the Cythera of the ancients. Under the denomination of dependencies on C., are Cerigotto, and several islets and rocks, among the most important of which are the Calami on the W coast; Diakofti on the E; the Ovo, Dragonares, and Kouphenisi on the S; and the Nautilus or Tracolithra, Porelli or Laguneres, and Porri or Trasonisi on the N.

CERIGOTTO, an island in the Grecian archipelago, 20 m. SSE of Cerigo. Area 12 sq. m. It is traversed by three mountain-ridges running NNE and SSW. Turcovigla, the highest summit in the central chain, has an alt. of 1,100 ft. above sea-level. There are four small ports on the island, but all are bad; the largest, called Potamo, is on the N coast. About 280 acres of the surface are under cultivation, and 164 in pasture.

CERILLY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Allier, arrond. of Montlucon. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,423; in 1841, 11,225. The town is prettily situated on the r. bank of the Marmande, 24 m. NNE of Montluçon, and 26 m. W of Moulins. Pop. in 1836, 2,320; in 1846, 2,387. It possesses manufactories of woollen fabrics, and some paper-mills; and has eight annual fairs. In the vicinity are the forges of Troncais.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Cote-d'Or, cant. of Laignes, 4 m. W of Chatillon. Pop. 476. It contains manufactories of sheet-iron and nails. Fairs are held here six times a-year.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Cerisiers, 19 m. from Joigny. Pop. 219. Gun-flints form an important article of export from this place. CERINA, CERINIA, CERIGNES, or DJERINA, a town and port of the island of Cyprus, cap. of a sanjak of the same name, 10 m. N of Nicosia, on the N coast, in N lat. 35° 19′ 30″, E long. 33° 15′. Pop. about 200. It is still surrounded by a dilapidated wall, and contains a church and a mosque. The port, which is defended by a square castle, is exposed on the N, and is capable of admitting only small vessels. The environs are extremely fertile, producing grain, cotton, olives, oranges, figs, &c., but frequently suffer from the ravages of locusts. To the W of the town are catacombs, now the only remains of the ancient Cerynia, supposed to have been founded by Cyrus; and about 3 m. to the SE, on the brow of a mountain, are the ruins of the ancient and magnificent convent of Bella-Paisa.
CERIONE, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 7 m. S of Biella. Pop. 1,550.
CE'RISANO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 7 m. WSW of Cosenza. Pop. 1,651. Silk is cultivated in the environs.

CE'RISIERS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, arrond. of Joigny. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,494; in 1841, 6,018. The town is situated in a narrow valley, 12 m. NNE of Joigny. Pop. 1,444. It contains extensive rope-works, and has a considerable trade in cider and rosin.
CE'RISY - BELLE - E'TOILE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Flers, on the r. bank of the Noireau, 18 m. N of Domfront. Pop. 1,362.

CE'RISY-LA-FORET, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Saint- Clair, 12 m. NE of Saint-Lo. Pop. 2,055. It contains an old church; and has extensive manufactories of ticking.

CE'RISY-LA-SALLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Manche, arrond. of Coutances.—The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,056; in 1841, 13,585.—The town is situated on an eminence on the r. bank of the Soulle, 8 m. E of Contances. Pop. 2,327. It contains a fine hall, and has a considerable trade in thread and ticking. Calico forms the chief article of local manufacture.

CERIZAY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, arrond. of Bressuire. The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,951; in 1841, 9,686. The town is situated on an affluent of the Sèvre-Nantaise. Pop. 1,230. It possesses manufactories of linen and tiles.
CERLIER. See ERLACH.

CERMIGNANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district of Citta-San-Angelo, on the summit of a hill 10 m. SE of Teramo. Pop. 2,140. It contains 2 churches, and a convent. A fair is held here once a-year.
CERNACHE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 5 m. S of Coimbra. Pop. 2,000.

CERNAMBITIGBA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, which issues from the Serra-dos- Orgâos, and forms one of the head-streams of the Guapi-Mirim. CERNAY, [GERM. Sennheim,] a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, arrond. of Belfort. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,690; in 1841, 14,297. The town is situated in a fine plain, on the l. bank of the Thurr, 21 m. NE of Belfort. Pop. in 1841, 3,433. It contains a handsome church; and has manufactories of calico, cloth, chintzes, ironmongery, and hydraulic and other machines, some bleacheries, paper and cotton spinning-mills, iron and copper foundries, &c. A fair is held once a-year. Coal is wrought in the environs.
CERNE. See MAURITIUS.

CERNE-ABBAS, a parish and market-town of 2,396. CER Dorset, 73 m. NNW of Dorchester, on the Cerne, in a valley surrounded by lofty chalk hills. Area 3,010 acres. Pop. 1,342. It has manufactories of coarse linen, gloves, parchment, leather, &c.; but malting and brewing form the principal local trade.
CERNE (NETHER), a parish of Dorset, 51 m. NNW of Dorchester. Area 1,330 acres. Pop. 71.

CERNETZ, or ZERNETZ, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, jurisdiction of the Unter-Engadin, on the l. bank of the Inn, at the confluence of the Spoil, 30 m. ESE of Chur. Pop. 450. It contains a Protestant church, the finest in the canton, the ruins of several fortresses, and several mineral springs and baths.
CERNEY (NORTH), a parish of Gloucestershire, 4 m. N of Cirencester. Area 4,000 acres. Pop. 668.
CERNEY (South), a parish of Gloucestershire, 4 m. SSE of Cirencester, on the Thames and Severn canal. Area 3,100 acres. Pop. 1,077.
CERNIER, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 6 m. NNW of Neufchatel, and 4 m. N of Vallangin. Pop. 500. It contains a mineral spring, and has an annual fair.

CERNIN, or SERNIN (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal, arrond. of Aurillac. The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,625; in 1841, 8,287. The town lies on the l. bank of the Doire, 11 m. N of Aurillac. Pop. in 1841, 3,046.—Also a commune in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Lanzès. Pop. 1,046.
CERNISI, an island of the Mediterranean, near the W coast of Sicily, in N lat. 37° 52', E long. 12° 26′.
CERNIZA, a town of Austria, in Illyria, in the gov. and 20 m. N of Trieste, and 8 m. SE of Gorizia.
CERNOBBIO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 4 m. N of Como, on the SW side of the lake of that name.
CERNUSCO-ASINARIO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 7 m. NE of Milan, on the N side of the canal of Martezana, which is here crossed by a fine bridge. Pop. 2,000.
CERO, or CERRO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 10 m. N of Verona. It has a monthly fair.
CEROCAHUI, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 150 m. SW of Chihuahua.
CERONS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Podensac, on the l. bank of the Garonne, opposite Cadellac, and 21 m. SE of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,373. The environs produce good white wine.
CERRALBO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 50 m. W of Salamanca, and in the partido of Lumbrales, near the r. bank of the Yeltes.

CERRETO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavora, district and 13 m. SE of Piedimonte, on the brow of Monte Matese, near the r. bank of the Cusano, and 22 m. ENE of Capua. Pop. 5,564. It is well-built, and contains a fine cathedral adorned with numerous paintings, a college, a seminary, and 3 convents. It possesses some manufactories of common cloth, and has five annual fairs. This town occupies the site of the ancient Cernetum of the Romans, near which Pyrrhus was defeated in 277 B.C. In 1656 it was half depopulated by the plague; and in 1688 it was to a great extent destroyed by an earthquake.—Also a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 14 m. NE of Spoleto, on the r. bank of the Nera. It contains some silk spinning-mills.
CERRETO-GUIDI, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. of Florence, 10 m. N of San-Miniato. Pop.
CERRIG-CEINWEN, a parish of Anglesey, 21/2 m. NW of Llangeffin. Pop. 550.
CERRIG-Y-DRUIDION, a parish of Denbighshire, on a branch of the Dee. Pop. 1,039.
CERRO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavora, district and 27 m. NW of Piedimonte. Pop. 1,870.
CERRO, a town of the island of Cuba, in the jurisdiction of Havannah. Pop. in 1841, 2,200.

CERRO-DA-BUENA, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, and district of Pelotas, bordered on the N by the Serra-dos-Tapes, and watered by the Pedras, Moreira, Paratinin, and São-Gonçalo.
CERRO-DA-VIGIA, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, near the Jaguarao. It is destitute of vegetation, and takes its name from its utility as a point of observation.

CERRO-DEL-ANDIVALO, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 27 m. N of Huelva, on a hill surrounded by the sierras of Almonaster, and on the road from Seville to Lisbon. Pop. 2,728. It is of ancient origin, and has a church and custom-house, but contains no remarkable buildings. The partido comprises 20 pueblos.
CERRO-DE-LA-GIGANTA, the most elevated section of the chain of mountains which runs through the peninsula of Old California. It is between 4,500 and 5,000 ft. in alt.

CERRO-DE-LA-SAL, a mountain of Peru, in the prov. of Pasco, which runs E, in S lat. 10° 50', from the E branch of the Andes, and separates the headstreams of the Pozuzu on the N from those of the Perene on the S.
CERRO-DE-POTOSI. See Potosi.
CERRO-DO-ROQUE, a group of mountains in Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, to the W of Lake Patos.
CERRO-GORDO, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 150 m. NNW of Durango.—Also a town in the prov. and 60 m. ESE of Guadalaxara.
CERRO-IRAJASSE', a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, comarca-das- Missões, near the source of the Rio-Negro.
CERRO - LARGO, a group of bare and lofty mountains in Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do- Rio-Grande, to the S of the Jaguarão, and in the territory claimed by Montevideo as neutral.
CERRO-PASCO. See Pasco.

CERRO-PELLADO, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, to the N of the Piratinin. CERRO - PELLADO - DA - ENCRUZILHADA, a group of mountains in Brazil, in the prov. of São- Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, between the Camacuan and Jacuhi.

CERROS, an island of the Pacific, near the W coast of Lower California, at the entrance of the bay of Sebastian Viscaino, in N lat. 28° 15', W long. 115° 10'. It is 30 m. in length, and 75 m. in circumference, and towards the S rises to a considerable height. Its coasts present several indentations, and it terminates in the SW in a bold promontory. It appears completely destitute of vegetation.
CERROY, a town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, on the l. bank of the Lunain, 14 m. W of Sens. It contains about 160 houses.

CERTA, or SERTAA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, on the r. bank of the Pera, 35 m. NE of Thomar. It contains a citadel, now in ruins, supposed to have been founded by Sertorius, a church, a convent, an hospital, an alms-house, and about 250 houses.

CERTALDO, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. of Florence, in the Valdelsa, or valley of the Elsa, a prov. and 36 m. NNE of Castellon-de-la-Plana, par- ~ CER small stream which flows into the Arno at Empoli 15 m. to the SE of C. The older part of the town, the C. of the 14th cent., is perched on the summit of a conical hill, on the highest point of which still stands the feudal stronghold of the Counts Alberti; but a new burgh has gradually been formed along the line of road which runs at the foot of the hill. A carriage-way has been formed in a series of traverses from the modern suburb to the old town; whose gateway opens on the brow of the hill, while its walls stand on the very edge of the steep descent. The town itself contains little more than one street paved with flag-stones. In its palmy days, the jurisdiction of the vicar of C. extended over a wide expanse of country; but the vicariate and its jurisdiction have been long abolished. C., however, retains a surer hold on the respect of the present and future generations as having been the home and the last restingplace of Boccacio, whose house is still shown here, and who lies buried in the church. An interesting account of C., and of such of its antiquities as relate to the father of Florentine prose, will be found in the Athenœum for 1849, No. 1,118.
CERTES, a hamlet and fishing-port of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and com. of Audenge, on an arm of the Bassin d'Arcachon. Pop. 200. In the environs are extensive saline marshes.
CERTOMONDO-IN-CAMPALDINO, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Arezzo, 11 m. N of Poppi, on the r. bank of the Arno. Several important fairs are held here.

CERTOSA-DI-FIRENZE, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 3 m. S of Florence, on Monte Acuto, at the confluence of the Ema with the Greve. It contains a magnificent chartreuse, erected in 1341, and adorned with numerous paintings and other objects of art.
CERTOSA-DI-PISA, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 7 m. E of Pisa, near Calci. It contains a chartreuse founded in 1366, and, next to that of Pavia, the finest in Italy.
CERTOSA-DI-PONTIGNANO, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 7 m. N of Sienna. The fine chartreuse founded here in 1343 was suppressed in 1810.
CERULIAN SPRINGS, a village of Trigg co., in the state of Kentucky, U. S., 11 m. N of Cadiz, on the Muddy Fork of Little river. It is a place of considerable resort during the summer season.
CERVALHOS, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre-Douro-e-Minho, 7 m. SSE of Oporto.

CERVARO, a river of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, which takes its rise on the confines of the prov. of Principato-Ultra, to the S of Monte Leone; makes a considerable circuit, and then takes a NE direction; passes Bovino; and after a total course of about 60 m. discharges itself into the lagune of Pantano-Salso, 18 m. ENE of Foggia.-Also a town, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra-di- Lavora, district and 27 m. SE of Sora, on a hill. Pop. 3,778. It contains 4 churches and an hospital, and has an annual fair. See also Cocuzzo.
CERVARO (PONTE), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, on the r. bank of the river of the same name which is here crossed by a bridge, 8 m. SE of Foggia.
CERVASCA, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 5 m. SW of Coni, near the l. bank of the Stura. Pop. 3,000.
CERVATOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Santander, partido of Reinosa, near the r. bank of the Cueza.
CERVEIRA. See VILLANOVA-DA-CERVEIRA'.

CERVERA, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the tido and 3 m. from San Mateo, on the l. bank of a small river. Pop. 1,536. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains a parish church and a customhouse. In the environs are the ruins of a castle, and a quarry of fine marble.-Also an ancient town in Catalonia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 35 m. E of Lerida, on a rising ground, near a small river of the same name, an affluent of the Segre. Pop. 5,312. It is defended by old walls; and contains a castle now in ruins, a parish church of Gothic architecture, a university, several colleges, 5 convents, and 2 hospitals. The streets present a gloomy aspect, but some of them are handsome and wellpaved. The surrounding district is extremely fertile, producing wine, grain, oil, and fruit. The partido comprises 68 pueblos. - Also a town of New Castile, in the prov. and 46 m. WNW of Toledo, partido and 7 m. N of Talavera-de-la-Reina. It contains some silk-spinning-mills and manufactories of parchment.—Also a town in the prov. and 25 m. SW of Cuenca, partido and 22 m. NE of Belmonte.- Also a cape of the coast of Valencia, prov. and 25 m. SSW of Alicante, in N lat. 38° 55'.

CERVERA-DEL-RIO-ALHAMA, an ancient town of Spain, in Old Castile, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 44 m. SE of Lograno, near the r. bank of the Alama. Pop. 3,690. It contains several parish churches, a convent, an hospital, &c., and has manufactories of coarse linen and soap. The partido comprises 12 pueblos.

CERVERA - DE - RIOPISUERGA, an ancient town of Spain, in Leon, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 60 m. N of Palencia, on a hill near the r. bank of the Pisuerga, which is here crossed by two bridges. Pop. 900. It contains a parish church, an hospital, a custom-house, and a convent, and on the summit of the hill are the ruins of a castle. The partido comprises 178 pueblos.
CERVETERI, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the legation and 15 m. WNW of Rome, on a small river, 3 m. above its entrance into the Mediterranean.

CERVI, or ELAFONISI, an island in the Grecian archipelago, 5 m. N of Cerigo, and divided from the mainland of the Morea by a very narrow and shallow channel of about 400 yards in width. It is a low sandy barren island; and in the time of the Roman empire was a peninsula connected with the mainland. It covers the bay of Vatika on the W; and there is good anchorage on its E side. It has recently attracted attention in consequence of having been claimed as a part of the Ionian republic.

CERVIA, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the legation and 14 m. SE of Ravenna, near the Adriatic, on which it has a small port, and with which it is connected by a navigable canal, in N lat. 44° 15′ 49″, E long. 12° 22′. Pop. 4,000. It is regularly built; and contains a cathedral, and several churches and convents. The trade consists chiefly in salt, the produce of the adjacent saline marshes, Fairs are held here twice a-year. To the W of the town is an extensive marsh named the Valle di Cervia.
CERVICALES, a group of islands in the Mediterranean, near the SE coast of the island of Corsica, in N lat. 41° 33'.
CERVICATE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 20 m. NNW of Cosenza. Pop. 1,400, chiefly Albanians.
CERVIERE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Noirétable, 26 m. NW of Montbrison. Pop. 487.
CERVIGNANO, a village of Illyria, in the gov. and 25 m. WNW of Trieste, and 4 m. NW of Aquileia, on an affluent of the Ausa. Pop. 1,407.
CERVILLEGO-DE-LA-CRUZ, a town of Spain, It contains a church, and 3 abbeys. CER in Leon, in the prov. and 29 m. SSW of Valladolid, partido of Medina-del-Campo.

CERVIN (MONT), or THE MATTERHORN, a remarkable pointed summit in the Pennine chain of the Alps, at the head of the Val Tournanche, on the S boundary of the canton of Valais, to the SSE of the Dent Blanche. Alt. 14,766 ft. [De Saussure], 14,750 ft. [Berchtold]. It is composed of metamorphic secondary rocks. The lower part is of the system of green slates which abound in this part of the Alps. The whole of its upper part appears to consist of a white quartzose slate. Professor Forbes pronounces the "unscaled and unscaleable pyramid" in which this noble mountain terminates, "beyond comparison the most striking object in the Alps." The outline here given is after his sketch of it from the Riffelberg, near Monte Rosa. The whole N face of Mount C., and the Dent d'Erin, to the W of it, is a united and inaccessible precipice descending into the glacier of Zmutt, which extends far to the W of both.—The Petit Mont C. and Col de St. Théodule, form a snowy chain of 11,000 ft. alt., connected with Mont C.

CERVINARA, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavora, district and 6 m. ESE of Arienzo, and 25 m. ESE of Capua. Pop. 6,224. It contains several handsome churches, and a convent. Fairs are held twice a-year.

CERVIONE, a canton, commune, and town of the island of Corsica, arrond. of Bastia. The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 2,884; in 1841, 3,056. The town is situated on a small river, in a fertile and picturesque locality, about 2 m. from the E coast, and 29 m. S of Bastia. Pop. in 1841, 1,536. Near it stands the very ancient church of Saint-Christine. It has a small port at the hamlet of Prunete.

CERVO, a river of Piedmont, which takes its rise in Monte Lasone, in the NW of the prov. of Biella; runs SE, passing Biella; crosses the N part of the prov. of Vercelli; receives the Elvo on the right; and after a total course of 45 m. falls into the Sesia at Vercelli.
CERVOLI, or CERBOLI, an islet of the Mediterranean, in the channel and 5 m. S of Piombino, between the island of Elba and the coast of Tuscany.
CERVON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of Corbigny, 27 m. SE of Clamecy. Pop. 2,108.
CERZA-MAGGIORE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, district and 7 m. SSE of Campobasso, at the foot of Monte Gizzio. Pop. 1,800. A fair is held once a-year.
CERZA-PICCOLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, district and 6 m. S of Sannio. Pop. 1,340.

CERZEDELLO, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, 60 m. from the coast, and 160 m. ESE of Belem, watered by the Gurupy. It is inhabited by Indians, whose chief employments consist in fishing and agriculture. The labours of the latter are performed by the women.
CERZETO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 15 m. NW of Cosenza, in a plain, watered by an affluent of the Crati. Pop. 1,223.
CESA, a village of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavora, district and 10 m. SW of Caserta. Pop. 1,620.
CESANA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, delegation and 11 m. SW of Belluno, on the l. bank of the Piave. Pop. 2,000.

CESANNA, or CEZANNA, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 15 m. SW of Susa, on the l. bank of the Dora-Ripuaria, at the E foot of Mont Genévre. Pop. 1,200. It contains a custom-house. Lime is extensively quarried in the environs. Napoleon ordered a road of the first class to be carried from this v. over the Col-de-Sestriere; but it never was completed. 659.

CESANO, a river of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation of Urbino-e-Pesaro, which takes its rise in Monte Catria, on the E side of the Apennines; passes Pergola and S. Lorenzo; and after a total course of about 36 m., in a generally NE direction, falls into the Adriatic 16 m. SE of Pesaro.
CESANO-MADERNO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 12 m. N of Milan, district and 3 m. SSE of Barlassina, on the l. bank of the Seveso. Pop. 1,133.

CESARE, or SESAR, a river of New Granada, in the intendancy of Magdalena, prov. of Santa Marta. It is formed by the junction of numerous streams which descend from the Sierras de Santa Marta and Pieja; runs SSW; passes through lakes Adentro and Tapatoza; and after a total course of 210 m., falls into the Magdalena on the r. bank, near Banca, and 16 m. NW of Tamalameque.
CESAREA. See Kaisarie'h.
CESAREO (PORTO), a small port of Naples, on the W coast of the prov. of Terra di Otranto, on the gulf of Taranto, in N lat. 40° 13', E long. 17° 56', 10 m. N by W of Gallipoli.

CESARIO (SAN), a town and circondario of Naples, in the prov. of Terra di Otranto, district and 3 m. S of Lecce. Pop. 3,449. There is a fine palace here. In the environs excellent tobacco is grown. 2,230.
CESARO, a town and circondario of Sicily, in the prov. of Messina, district and 21 m. ESE of Mistretta. Pop. 2,000.
CE'SEMBRE, a small island of France, belonging to the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, com. and 3 m. NNW of Saint Malo. It is defended by two forts.

CESENA, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the leg. and 12 m. SE of Forli, on the r. bank of the Savio, at the foot of the hill Garampi. Pop. 14,000. It is a well-built town, and is the see of a bishop. It possesses in the Biblioteca Malatestiana, a library peculiarly rich in MSS. Flax, silk, and vines, are extensively cultivated in the environs; and important fairs are held here in June and August. The market is one of the best in Italy. A handsome bridge of 3 arches crosses the Savio.—This town was the birthplace of J. A. Braschi, who was elevated to the popedom under the name of Pius VI., and likewise of his successor, Barnobe Chiarimenti, afterwards Pope Pius VII. ČESENATICO, a port in the Adriatic, in the territory of the foregoing town, from which it is 9 m. distant. Pop. 3,000.
CESPEDOSA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and II. CET 20 m. SSE of Salamanca, on a hill near the r. bank of the river Tormes. Pop. 788.
CESSENON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Hérault, cant. of Saint-Chinian. Pop. 2,163.
CESSEY-LES-VITTEAUX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Vitteaux. Pop. 84.
CESSFORD, a village in the p. of Eckford, in Roxburghshire, 6 m. NE of Jedburgh. Pop. 150.
CESSIE'RES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Anizy-le-Chateau, 7 m. W of Laon. Pop. 600.
CESSIEUX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. and 4 m. W of La-Tour-du- Pin, near the r. bank of the Bourbre. Pop. 2,012.
CESSNA, a township of Hardin co., in the state of Ohio, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 258.
CESSON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 4 m. E of Rennes, on the r. bank of the Vilaine, at the highest point to which that river is navigable. Pop. 2,366.
CESSY-LES-BOIS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, cant. of Douzy. Pop.
CESTAYROLS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. and 8 m. NE of Gaillac. Pop. 1,148.

CESTONA (SANTA CRUZ DE), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Guipuscoa, 12 m. SW of Saint Sebastian, on a plain near the r. bank of the Urola or Urula. Pop. 1,100. There are warm ferruginous springs here, which are much frequented. The bathing establishment has accommodation for about 140 inmates. Rock crystals and jasper abound in the neighbouring mountains of Erchina and Izarriz. In the neighbourhood, on the Urola, is an iron-forge and foundry, which produces good iron, but at a cost 50 per cent. higher than that of English iron.

CESTOS, or Sestros, a river on the Grain coast of Africa, which has its embouchure about 30 leagues from Grand Bassa point, in S lat. 5° 26' 25", W long. 9° 34′ 45″. It is navigable for boats as far as the king's town, which is about 4 leagues from the entrance. Its bar has from 11 to 12 ft. of water upon it; and the roadstead gives the best shelter on this part of the coast. Palm-oil is exported in considerable quantity from this river.
CETARA, a town of Naples, in Principato-Citra, 3 m. SW of Salerno, on the gulf of Salerno. Pop.
CETINA, a village of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 15 m. W of Calatayud, partido of Ateca, on the r. bank of the Xalon.
CETINA. See CETTINA.
CETON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Le Theil, 24 m. SSE of Mortagne-sur-Huine. Pop. 3,559. Cotton - stuffs are manufactured here.
CETONA, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. of Arezzo, 6 m. WSW of Chiusi. Pop. 2,359.
CETRARO, a town and port of Naples, in Calabria-Citra, district and 12 m. NNW of Paola, on the Mediterranean. The anchovy fishery is actively engaged in by the seamen of this port.

CETTE, or SE'TE, a canton, commune, town, and port of France, in the dep. of Hérault, at the extremity of a long narrow tongue of land which separates the Etang de Thau from the Mediterranean, 18 m. SSW of Montpellier. A town called Sita existed in 837 at a little distance from the site of C.; but the present is a modern town, which owes its existence to the construction of the canal of Languedoc, and is daily rising into greater importance as forming the best port of departure for Algeria. The canal opens into the etang, from which a cut has been made into the Mediterranean; and the port of C. is formed by two piers which extend into the sea on each side of the mouth of this cut or outer canal. On the l. or W side of the entrance to the harbour is a fixed light, elevated 84 ft., in N lat. 43° 23′ 48", E long. 3° 41′ 52". The harbour has from 16 to 19 ft. water. The approach to C. is by a long causeway, called the Peyrade, carried on 52 arches along the strip of land between the etang and the sea. The town itself is well-built, but is altogether a maritime place; the quays, and the canal, which traverses the town, occupying a large portion of the narrow peninsula. The older or W part of the town is built on the side of Mont Set, the Setius Mons of the Romans, a calcareous eminence which forms the headland of C. The E part is built upon piles; the two parts are united by a bridge. A railway connecting C. with Montpellier was opened in 1839. Steam-vessels of 600 tons burden, and 300 horse-power, sail from C. on the 2d, 12th, and 22d of each month for Algiers. The manufactures of C. consist of salt, snuff, glass, raisin-syrup, refined sugar, perfumes, liqueurs, and cooperage. Its exports are wool, cheese, almonds, dried fruits, flour, olive-oil, soap, wood, marble, coal, verdigris, iron, brandy, and wine. Extensive cod, oyster, sardine, and tunny fisheries, are conducted from C. About 36,000 tuns of wine, and 4,000 tuns of brandy, are annually exported from this port; but it is known that C. carries on an extensive manufacturing and exporting trade in false wines of all kinds and qualities [Athenœum, 1849, p. 622.] In 1841, the exports by coasting-vessels amounted to 592,907 quintals, of which Marseilles took 134,838 q., and Rouen 104,688 q. Of these exports, 249,827 q. were in wine; 78,427 q. in salt; 22,308 q. in grain and flour; 10,067 q. in fish; and 155,533 q. in brandy. The imports amounted to 296,460 q. The total movement of the port consisted of <math>2,989</math> vessels <math>= 301,803</math> tons. Of this <math>872 = 91,444</math> tons entered coastwise. The entries from foreign ports were 592 vessels = 49,829 tons, of which 161 = 18,120 were from Algeria, and tons. The town is frequented for its sea-baths and sand-baths; but the surrounding scenery is sterile, desolate, and monotonous. Pop. in 1831, 10,638; in 1841, 13,413. Spain, Sardinia, Sweden, Denmark, and the United States, have consuls at this port.— Millin, Voyage dans les Dep. du Midi de la France.— B. Siau, Port de C., 8vo. 1836. - Magasin Pittoresque. 1848.

CETTIGNE, or TZETINIE, a small town or village rather of 19 or 20 houses, in the territory of Montenegro. It lies in a small valley about 21 m. in length, and from 4 m. to 3 m. in breadth; and contains the palace of the vladika, a long white-washed building of a single story over the ground floor, like a barrack, surrounded by a wall. Above the palace, on the slope of the hill, stands the convent, in which are kept the treasure, and the pontifical robes and mitres, of the vladika. The plain of C. is mostly grass land. The portion which is cultivated produces a little stunted maize, and some potatoes and cabbages.

CETTINA, a river of Dalmatia, which rises in four sources in a valley separating Mount Dinara from the Gniath range. Its head-streams unite at Vrilo- Cettina, and it then flows through the plains of Sign and Trigl, and falls into the sea at Almissa, which stands on its l. bank. It is the Tilurus of the ancients.

CEUTA, a presidio belonging to Spain, on the N coast of Marocco, the capital or seat of government of the four Spanish presidios on the African coast. The town is built on a small peninsula about 3 m. in length from WSW to ENE, which forms the SE point of the strait of Gibraltar, and lies a little to the E of the meridian of that fortress. This peninsula rises to the height of 300 or 400 ft. above sea-level, and consists of a series of seven small cones or hills, united at their base, which is connected with the African continent by a piece of low ground. The narrow land-front, consisting of two or three lines of badly flanked works, one behind another, has a strong profile, and a wet ditch which converts the peninsula into an island and is crossed by a drawbridge. On this low ground the town (C) is built; and to the NE of it the peninsula spreads out around a small bay opening into the mouth of the strait, and rises into the headland called the Almina [El Minah], of which the central point (A) is called the Acho. Pleasant suburbs are scattered over the Almina, where every house has its own garden and well; and the orange, citron, pomegranate, and vine are cultivated for the supply of Gibraltar. On the summit of the Acho, which is the most elevated point of the Almina, is a watchtower, and a strong fort with ample intrenchments. Between the Punta-Santa-Catalina, (a) or N point of the Almina, and the islets of El-Campo, (b) is the anchorage of the lesser bay of C., having from 8 to 13 fath. water. The great bay of C. is formed by Almina point (c) on the N, and Cape Negro on the S.; and is 11 m. in length between these points, and 3 m. in depth. Ships of all sizes may have anchorage in the requisite depth of water, in any part of this bay near the shore; but there are some spots of foul ground in it; and it is not protected from the Levanter or E wind. The principal mole (f) is at the part which divides the city from the suburb, (d) between the bastions of San Francisco and San Sebastian. The hills above the town are in part covered with shady woods, while in other parts rows of tall aloes extend along their summit. The port of C. is but of moderate depth, and is not regarded as a safe harbour. STRAITS OF GIBRALTAR. The pop. of C., exclusive of the garrison, is 8,000. It is the see of a bishop, who is a suffragan of Seville; and has an ecclesiastical and military tribunal. Its two convents are now deserted; but it has some handsome public buildings, several churches, capacious magazines, and water-tanks. The military commander is also political governor of the place, and of the other three presidios. C. obtains all its supplies from Spain, with the exception of an occasional bullock smuggled across the lines. Even water is often carried over from Estapona. The Arab videttes (e e e) keep a strict watch on C., and suffer no intercourse to take place betwixt it and the interior: so that its territory does not extend beyond reach of its guns; and, in a political point of view, this possession has perhaps ever been a useless burden in the hands of Spain. It has been useful, however, as a place of exile for political offenders,-a kind of state-prison for Spain; and it is capable of being converted into an impregnable fortress, and even in its present state is well-secured against a coup-de-main. A considerable number of criminals are kept here as galleyslaves in chains, hard-worked, half-starved, and nearly naked.—The Periplus of Antoninus distinguishes the Ad Septem Fratres from Abyla. Of these two localities, the first was the site of the present town; the second was the eastern extremity of the little peninsula which it overlooks. The name Septa appears for the first time in Isidore of Spain, who mentions it again in his Origines almost in the same terms with Pomponius Mela. Lastly, in the notice of the Emperor Leo, its name occurs among the towns of Mauritania Tingitana, of which he gives a list under an analogous Greek form. Septa, pronounced Sebta by the Arabs, who have no p sound in their language, has been changed into Ceuta by the Spaniards. C. sustained a part of considerable importance during the eight centuries of the Moorish dominion in Spain. It was with Julianus or Ælianus, Count and Governor of C., that Moussa-ben-Nosseir, Emir of Maghreb, for the khalif of Damascus, first contracted alliance; and his Arab and Berber conquerors passed over to the peninsula on board vessels from Tangier and Ceuta. From this period we find continual mention of C. [Septah] in the Mussulman chroniclers. It shared the fortunes of the Almohades, and the several Mussulman sovereigns who in turn became masters of Maghreb; until 1409, or, according to some, 1415, when it was conquered by the Portuguese. After the revolution of 1640, by which Portugal detached itself from Castile, the Spaniards continued masters of C. notwithstanding the repeated claims and pretensions urged by the princes of the house of Braganza. It has since remained in the power of Spain, though many successive attempts to re-seize it were made by the scherifs of Marocco, who, in 1697, and at several subsequent intervals, attacked it with numerous forces.

CEVA, a town of Sardinia, in Piedmont, 10 m. ESE of Mondovi, at the junction of the Cevetta with the Tanaro, and at an alt. of 984 ft. above sea-level. It contains a church, a communal college, and several convents; and has manufactures of silk, and some iron-forges. A highly esteemed cheese called rubiola is made here. The surrounding territory abounds in white truffles.—C. is a place of great antiquity. It was the Ceba of the Romans; and its cheese is praised by Pliny. It was surrounded by walls, which were nearly all thrown down by a great inundation of the Tanaro in 1584. It was taken by the French in 1796, and again in 1800.

CE'VENNES, a lofty mountain-chain in the S of France, the Cebenna, Gebenna, and Cemenii Montes, of the Romans. They run from SSW to NNE, from the Canal-du-Midi, or at a point to the W of Castelnaudary in the dep. of Aude, in N lat. 43° 17', to the Canal-du-Centre, at a point SE of Mont-Cenis, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, in N lat. 46° 40', traversing the departments of Tarn, Aude, Hérault, Aveyron, Gard, Lozère, Ardèche, Haute-Loire, Puyde-Dôme, Loire, Rhone, Allier, and Saone-et-Loire. They occupy a central position between the systems of the Pyrenees and the Alps; and are regarded by some geographers as forming the extreme W section of the Alpique system. On the S they are connected with the contreforts of the Pyrenees by the Col-de-la-Narouse, near Castelnaudary; and on the N with the small chain of the Côte-d'Or. They separate the basins of the Garonne and the Loire, from those of the Rhone and the Saône; and form the water-shed between the streams flowing into the Atlantic and those flowing into the Mediterranean. The length of the chain is about 320 m. The C. assume various denominations in different parts of their development. In the dep. of Aude and of Hérault, they are called the Montagnes Noires, or Black mountains; in the dep. of Tarn, of Aveyron, Hombres Firmas, Nivellement barom. des C., 1832, 8vo. めいもの めいがく かいがく ステル・スティー・スティー・スティー・スティー・スティー・スティー・スティー・スティー and part of Hérault, they bear the name of the Montagnes de l'Epinous or de l'Espinouse; in those of Aveyron and Gard, they are called the Garrigues; in Lozère, the Montagnes du Gévaudan, or the Cévennes Proper; in Ardèche, they are known as the Montagnes du Vivarais; in the Rhone, as the Montagnes du Lyonnais; and in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, they are called the Montagnes-du-Charolais or Maconnaise. The culminating points in the Cévennes are the Pic Montano in the Montagnes Noires, alt. 1,040 metres, or 3,412 ft.; the Lozère in the Gevaudan, at the source of the Tarn, in N lat. 44° 45', alt. 1,480 metres, or 4,855 ft., according to one admeasurement, but 1,490 metres, or 4,888 ft., according to another; Mont Mezenc or Mezen, at the source of the Lignon, in the Vivarais, alt. 1,774 metres, or 5,820 ft.; the Gerbier-de-Jone to the S of Mont Mezenc, at the source of the Loire, in N lat 44° 45', alt. 1,562 metres, or 5,124 ft.; the Tarare, alt. 1,450 metres, or 4,757 ft.; Mont Pilat, or Pilet, in the Lyonnais, to the E of Saint-Etienne, alt. 1,364 met., or 4,475 ft.—The heights of the Cote-d'Or, the plateau of Langres in the dep. of Haute-Marne, and the Faucille mountains in the dep. of Vosges, form the connecting link between the N section of the C. and the chain of the Vosges. From Mont Lozère several branches are thrown off towards Auvergne, connecting the C. with the volcanic groups of that district.- Granite is the predominating rock in the C.; but in some parts the rocks are calcareous, as in the neighbourhood of Alais, in the dep. of Gard. Towards the base of the chain, considerable beds of slate are found; and between it and the Rhone are large deposits of coal.—The prevailing character of the C. is that of a rugged barren district, in which great industry alone can struggle successfully against the natural poverty of the soil. The country known as the Cevennes in the earlier history of France, formed part of the prov. of Languedoc, and comprised the Gévaudan, the Vivarais, and the Velay. Its principal towns were Mende, Florac, Langogne, Marvejols, Viviers, Saint-Andeol, Joyeuse, Aubenas, Tournon, Annonay, Le Puy, Yssengeaux, Le Monestier, Le Vigau, Alais, and Uzes. The fastnesses of the C. afforded refuge to the Huguenots in the religious wars of France. The lozenge-shaped district, called the Higher C., is nearly defined by four streams,—the two branches of the river Tarn, and the two Gardons. It is a country difficult of access, full of lofty peaks and ridges, ravines and mountain-torrents, passes and defiles. Of its three stages or plateaux, the highest is covered with forests; the next is pasture; while on the lowest corn is grown. The climate is severe in winter; stormy and uncertain in summer. Scattered among the gorges, overhanging the streams, and perched upon the rocks of the wilderness, are five or six hundred villages or hamlets, accessible only by narrow and difficult mountain-paths. The pop., almost entirely Protestant to this day, amounts to about 30,000. Their habits are very simple. They live on rye, and the chestnuts of their woods, which they eat boiled in milk. In summer, they feed their flocks; in winter, when the snow confines them for six months to their cottages, they prepare the wool. The children spin, the women card, and the men weave; and every house is a little manufactory of serges and coarse cloths. The Lower C. are bare, grey, and arid; still further southward are the parched plains of Languedoc, producing the olive, the vine, and the fig-tree. Connected with the C. was another Protestant district, 'the Hundred Churches of Dauphiné,' lying chiefly in the valley of the Drome, which extends towards the Alps from the l. bank of the Rhone.— -Coquerel, Hist. des eglises du désert chez les protestants de France, 1841, 2 vols. 8vo.-Malte Brun.

CEVIO, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, in the Valle-Maggia, 15 m. NW of Bellinzone, on the l. bank of the Rovana, a little above its junction with the Maggia, at an alt. of 1,408 ft. above sea-level.
CEVOLI, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. of Pisa, 2 m. SE of Lari, near the Cascina. Pop. 1,172.
CEVOLLA. See CEBOLLA.

CEYLON, a large and valuable island, situated at the W entrance of the bay of Bengal, between 5° 55' and 9° 50' N lat., and 79° 42' and 82° 5' E long. On the NW it is separated from the Coromandel coast of India by the gulf of Manaar, and on the N by Palk's strait; and it is distant about 160 m. from Cape Comorin. On the E and S it is washed by the Indian ocean. From Point-Palmyra, its northern extremity, in N lat. 9° 48' 52", E long 80° 15' 58" [Higgs], to Dondra-head, its southern, in N lat. 5° 55′ 15″, E long. 80° 38′ 48" [Twynam], the extreme length is about 270 m. Its breadth is unequal, varying from 40 to 150 m. Its circuit is about 780 m. The whole island has very much the shape of a pear, with the larger end towards the S. "Our homely and flesh-pot-loving predecessors in possession, the Dutch, likened it, after their manner, to various gastronomic delicacies. To one mynheer it seemed as a plover's egg; to another, it assumed the likeness of a leg of mutton; whilst a third elevated the ancient Taprobane to a comparison with a Westphalian ham, and found 'the ham's knuckle' in the rocky ridge by which the island approaches the great Indian continent." [Colon. Mag.] Its superficial area is variously computed at from 20,770 to 24,664 sq. m.—In Sanscrit writings this island is called Lanka, that is 'the holy' or 'resplendent.' The name from which the modern one is formed is Singhaladwipa, that is 'the Country of lions,' although the lion has not been found upon it, at least in modern times. The Arabs call it Serendib; the Portuguese, Selan. To the Greeks and Romans it was known by the name Taprobane. Coasts and Physical features.] From the sea this island presents a fresher green, and more fertile appearance, than most parts of the Coromandel coast, the nearest point of which is about 65 m. distant. But the aspect of the island depends materially on the quarter whence it is approached. The E shore, particularly towards the S, is bold, rocky, and in many parts extremely picturesque. On the SW and S, where the mountains of the interior approach the coast, a gentle declivity extends from their base to the sea, ultimately terminating in a level beach fringed with groves of cocoa-nut trees. On the W and NW, the coast is uniformly low and indented with numerous bays and inlets. The N coast is also of a similar character, but more cleared, better cultivated, and at the farthest extremity broken into numerous islands, which together assume the form of a peninsula. [Parl. paper. From a point on the coast of the Indian peninsula, on the S side of the river Vaygaru in Madura, all the way to the coast of C., there is a succession of banks and shoals known by the name of Adam's bridge. The natives—who believe that their island was the shoals is called the Manaar-passage, from an island and rocky, and the water deep. The N and NE are flat, and indented with lagunes, and inlets from the sea, several of which form small harbours; but the it is impossible for vessels of a large size to approach it, and the only two passages through the chain of shoals and islands just described have only the one 4, and the other 6 ft. of water. The principal harbours for large ships are Trincomalee on the NE, and Point-de-Galle on the extreme S. The maritime districts, comprising about one-half of the S, E, and W provinces, are flat; the N province, and the N portion of the E province, are throughout low and bare in surface. The interior of the island abounds with steep and lofty mountains, covered with forests and full of almost impenetrable jungles; but these do not approach nearer the coast than 30 or 40 m., except on the S, where there is a low range approaching the coast. This central country-of which the central point may be placed in 7° 3' N lat., and 80° 46' E long., which comprises about a seventh or an eighth of the whole area of the island—is intersected by deep and impassable ravines, and clothed with thick jungle or forest. The most lofty range of the interior mountains attains an elevation of above 7,000 ft., and averages 2,000 ft. The highest continuous portions of table-land occur between Maturatta and Fort M'Donald, and adjoining Nuwera-Elliya. The ranges do not observe great uniformity of direction. In Dumbera they run NNE and SSW; in Oava, in various directions. No connected range of mountains can be traced; but a succession of isolated hills rise rapidly over each other, from the level belt of coastland to the centre of the island, where various lofty mountains of fantastic shapes rear their peaks to the height of more than 7,000 ft., giving a picturesque and singular effect to the whole. The hills of which this interior mass is composed, are for the most part either conical or oblong, but in a few instances they stretch into table-land of considerable extent, encircled by still greater elevations, and forming, as it were, an extensive basin. The valleys of the interior are narrow, confined, extremely tortuous in their windings, and often form the bed of a water-course; the soil, being composed of the detritus of the neighbouring mountains washed down by the torrents, and of an accumulation of vegetable matter, is exceedingly fertile, and owing to the facility for irrigation, is much used for the raising of rice. The hills are seldom brought under cultivation, though well adapted for every description of tropical produce. From the rapidity of the descent towards the low grounds, there are no natural lakes in the interior, and seldom any extent of marshy ground, because, however deep the valley, the water is always sure to find an outlet; but, during heavy rains, the rivulets rush down with such impetuosity as to inundate the level grounds along their course, and this abundant supply of moisture, aided by the power of a tropical sun, speedily gives birth to such a density of vegetation as to render the uncleared valleys nearly impassable. On the next 2,000 ft., which makes 6,000, the trees become smaller; beautiful herbaceous plants, ferns from 3 inches in height to tree-ferns of 20 ft. in height, are here abundant; and it is here that the tree-rhododendron first makes its appearance. The elevation of the next 2,000 ft. is remarkable for the profusion of such plants as balsams, palms, ferns, and also for its great variety of sufruticose acanthaceæ. At 6,200 ft. many European vegetables grow freely; but peaches are evergreen and worthless, and the cherry flowers but the fruit never comes to perfection. Except in the vicinity of the principal villages or lines of communication, the flat belt of land which stretches in every direction to the sea-coast is also in a great measure covered with forests, and frequently wet and marshy, owing to the streams from the high grounds overspreading the surface during heavy rains, and evaporation being retarded by the dense foliage which excludes the solar rays. In such spots, the soil is covered to the depth of several inches with the debris of vegetation in various stages of decomposition, and the air becomes loaded with offensive effluvia, while universal gloom and stillness indicate the locality as unfavourable to animal life. The traveller often finds himself enveloped in such woods for many miles; occasionally he meets with open glades free from forest or underwood, but these are of comparatively limited extent, and equally tenantless. Though the low grounds are of this description in many parts, in others they present a very different aspect. As the sea-coast is approached, indications of life and industry become more frequent, and cultivation is found rapidly advancing under the exertions of an increasing population. Along the banks of many of the streams, particularly at their embouchure, numerous villages are to be met with; and in the districts of Galle and Colombo, which are the most populous, the inhabitants average from 150 to 160 per sq. m. [Parl. paper.] The rivers, generally speaking, are small, and have their courses from an elevated tract of country at the base of the mountains. To this, however, there is one exception: the Mahavillaganga, which rises on the N side of Adam's peak, has a course of about 35 m. direct N to Kandy; thence it bends to the E for about 35 m.; after which it flows N to the sea, a distance of about 70 m. more, and empties itself by two mouths into the bay of Trincomalee. It is the largest river of the island; but except for conveying timber-rafts, it is of little value to trade. It drains about two-thirds of the surface of the Central province. Between Kandy and Bintenné, at the foot of the mountains, a distance of 30 m., it has a fall of 1,000 ft. At Bintenné, when of a medium height, it has a depth of 5 ft., and a breadth of 540 ft.—The Kalani-ganga, which flows W, and falls into the sea at Colombo, is navigable for about 50 m., to Ratnapura, and might be cleared for boats to beyond Ambagamma, whence it might be connected by a tunnelled canal of about 8 m. in length with Kettulgalle, at the highest navigable point of the Kalani.—The country extending N from the foot of the hills to Trincomalee is the most deficient in water. Recent coast formations.] The flatness of the districts bordering on the sea has occasioned the formation of extensive lagunes, which frustrate to a considerable extent the intercourse of the maritime districts. "The rivers, which flow E and W, on both sides of the island, are met at their junction with the ocean by a powerful current which flows from the N; and all their deposits, instead of being carried directly out to sea, are heaped up in the form of immense bars and sand-banks, which gradually extend towards the S, and force the rivers to turn in that direction in order to find an outlet. The formation of these bars when once commenced proceeds with great rapidity, owing to the accumulation of sand thrown up during the monsoons, when a furious surf beats full upon the shore for many months uninterruptedly, till at last a long stripe of land is formed which stretches from N to S, behind which the river bends, forming a narrow lake varying from 4 m. to 2 m. or 3 m. broad, and sometimes extending for 20 m. along the shore between the mainland and the new marine formation. Occasionally, the violence of the monsoon drives in such quantities of sand and fresh deposit as to close up the ordinary embouchure of these channels, and the river, swollen by its confinement, breaks a fresh opening for itself in some other direction, and not unfrequently leaves its ancient channel converted into an absolute lake without any opening whatsoever. In process of time these sand-banks become covered with vegetable life; herbaceous plants, shrubs, and, finally, trees peculiar to saline soils successively make their appearance; and, as these decay, their decomposition degenerates a sufficiency of soil when mixed with the upper coating of sand to sustain continued vegetation. The process of this conversion may be seen in all its stages at various points of the coast. The margin of sand nearest to the water is first taken possession of by some saline grasses and succulent plants, such as the glassworts, which are provided with penetrating and tenacious roots; and a breakwater, as it were, being thus secured, the drier sand above is next seized upon by the goat's-foot convolvulus, which seems to be always the first to make its appearance. It covers the surface with its purple flowers, and its procumbent branches shooting in all directions send down roots from every joint that serve to give the sand its first solidity. Along with the convolvulus grows a creeping bean with large pods, a fleshy plant [Hydrophalax maritima] called moodoo-gaeta-kola by the natives, a species aristolochia, and a strong coarse grass called moharawana, some crotolarias, and the sayan mal or choya, [Hedyotis umbellata,] which is used as a substitute for madder. All these have creeping roots, and, striking them directly downwards, they prevent the sand from being displaced by the wind. The bank thus protected from the action of the air above and of the water at its base, other herbaceous plants soon cover it in quick succession, and give the entire surface its first dense carpet of vegetation. In the next stage low shrubs make their appearance, their seed being drifted by the waves, and taking ready root when they rest. The foremost of these are the mangroves, of which there are five or six varieties on this coast, all growing luxuriantly within the actual wash of the sea; whilst, a little retired from the influence of the tides, the undisturbed and marshy spots are covered with tamarisks, acanthus, and several varieties of hebiscus, and the dry sands above are taken possession of by acacias, Salvadora Persica—the true mustard-tree of Scripture—which here attains a height of 40 ft., ixoras, and the numerous family of cassias. Lastly, after a sufficiency of earth has been formed by frequent successions of their less important predecessors, the ground becomes covered by trees of ampler magnitude, most of which are found upon the adjacent shore of the mainland; the margosa, [Azadirachta Indica,] from whose seed the natives express a valuable oil; the timbiri, [Diaspyros embriopteris,] with whose glutinous nuts the fishermen bark their nets; the cashu nut; the palu, one of the most valuable timber-trees of the N province; and the wood apple, [Feronia elephantum,] whose fruit is regarded by the Singhalese as an unfailing specific for dysentery. But the most important fact connected with these recently-formed portions of land is their extraordinary suitability for the growth of the cocoanut, which affects the sea air, and never succeeds at any distance from it; and which at the same time requires a light and sandy situation, and the constant presence of water in large quantities. All these essentials are combined in the sea-belts I have described, lying as they do between the ocean on the one side and the fresh-water lakes formed by the great rivers on the other, and presenting every requisite of soil and surface. It is along a sand formation of this description, about 40 m. long, and from 1 m. to 3 m. broad, that the recent thriving cocoa-nut plantations have been commenced at Batticaloa. At Calpentyn, on the W coast, a similar formation has been taken advantage of for the same purpose. At Jaffna the same peculiarities of soil and locality have been equally seized on for this promising cultivation; and generally along the whole sea-border of C. to the S and W, the shore, for the breadth of 1 or 2 m., exhibits one almost continuous grove of the cocoanut palms." [Sir J. E. Tennent.] The recent formation of cocoa-nut estates has taken place chiefly in the N and E provinces, where the pop. is rare, and the lands unoccupied; but towards the W and S some flourishing plantations have been simultaneously opened by Europeans. Geology.] This great island presents much the appearance of having once formed part of the continent of India, and there is a striking similarity in the nature of its principal rocks, which are chiefly primitive, to those of the mainland immediately opposite to it. Dr. Davy's valuable work affords the following interesting and scientific description. "In C., nothing is to be observed of that order of succession of rocks that occurs in Saxony and England, and many other parts of Europe. Uniformity of formation is the most remarkable feature in the geological structure of the island. The whole of C., with few exceptions, consists of primitive rock unconnected with any other class of rocks, exclusive of those of very recent formation. Another remarkable geological circumstance is, that though the varieties of primitive rock are extremely numerous-almost infinite—yet the species are very few and seldom welldefined. The most prevailing species is granite or gneiss; the more limited are quartz rock, hornblende rock, and dolomite rock, and a few others which may be considered under the head of embedded minerals. The varieties of granite and gneiss are innumerable, passing often from one into another, and assuming appearances for which, in small masses, it would be difficult to find out appropriate names, depending on composition, and the proportions of the elements, or addition of new ingredients. Regular granite is not common; graphic granite still rarer; it occurs at Trincomalee. Neither is signite common; it occurs in the Candyan provinces. Well formed gneiss is more abundant than granite; it frequently consists of white felspar and quartz in a finely crystallized state, with layers of black mica, containing numerous crystals of light coloured garnets. A similar rock is found on the opposite continent, in the mountains at Cotallum, and affords one amongst other evidences of a conformity, if not identity, in geological character. Both the granite and gneiss of C., are much modified by an excess or deficiency of one or other of the ingredients. When quartz abounds in a fine granular state, the rock looks like sandstone; when felspar, or adularia, abounds, it acquires a new external character. This variety is common; and in some places it contains so much of these minerals that it may be called adularia, or felspar rock. When mica prevails in gneiss-which is rare-it acquires not only 422 CEYLON. slate. The more limited varieties of primitive rocks, as quartz, hornblende, and dolomite rock, seldom occur in the form of mountain-masses. The rocks of recent formation are limestone and sandstone. The former is confined to the N shore of the island, where it appears to be still forming in the coral-shallows of the adjoining sea. The other-sandstone-a rock of pretty general occurrence along the shore of the island, which it may be said to surround by an interrupted chain chiefly between high and low water mark." The further detailed description of these rocks given by this scientific observer, and his account of the rich variety of beautiful minerals abounding in C. will be found highly interesting and instructive. Climate.] This island, being open to the influence of the two monsoons which alternately prevail in the Indian seas, includes a greater variety of climate than is to be found in any territory of equal extent on the continent of India. On the W side, the rains prevail in May, June, and July,-the season in which they are felt on the Malabar coast; and during their continuance, the N parts of the island are generally dry. In the months of October and November, when the opposite or NE monsoon sets in on the Coromandel coast, it is the N part of C. which is affected, and scarcely any impression is made on the S. But the NE winds are dry, and give an arid appearance to the country over which they blow, which contrasts with the luxuriant verdure of the S and W districts. The driest divisions are those between the range of the two monsoons.—The climate is unhealthy in the interior; but every point of the sea-coast that is cleared of wood, and drained and cultivated, is salubrious: Colombo and its neighbourhood, being the best cultivated, are particularly so. It is now, indeed, wellknown, that healthiness of climate does not depend on parallels of latitude; that a healthy state of the human constitution is not incompatible with the most intense heat; but, on the contrary, is more adapted to an equatorial than a polar climate, provided the atmosphere be not overcharged with humidity. Where the country is depopulated and overgrown with jungle, endemic fever returns at certain seasons; and by some, it is alleged that the climate is excessively monotonous and enervating. Though the extreme heat on the sea-coast of C. is never so great as on the adjacent continent of India, yet as the therm. does not fall so low during the cold season, the mean temp. throughout the year is much the same, being about 80°. To the feelings, however, C. is decidedly the coolest of the two, and the heat is rarely complained of as oppressive. The range of temp. is highest on the N and E coast, owing to the SW monsoon becoming heated in its transit across the island, and the NE monsoon not being always divested of the heat acquired in passing over the arid plains of India before reaching the island. In the interior the temp. depends of course materially on elevation. At Colombo, the mean temp. is 81°, and the daily variation of the temp. does not exceed 3°, while the annual range of the therm. is from 76° to 86½°. At Galle, the mean daily variation is 4°, and the annual range from 71° to 87°. At Jaffnapatam, the mean daily variation is 5°, and the annual range from 70° to 90°. At Trincomalee, on the E coast, the greatest daily variation is 17°, and the annual range from 74½° to 91½°. At Kandy, which is 1,676 ft. above sea-level, the mean temp. is <math>73\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}</math>; the mean daily variation 6°, and the annual range from 66° to 86°; and higher up the hills, at Nuwera-Elliya, a military convalescent station, the mean daily variation is as high as 11°, while the annual range of the therm. is from <math>35\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}</math> to <math>80\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}</math>. Along the sea-coast, the daily variations of temp. rarely exthe appearance, but very much the structure of mica | ceed 10° or 12°, and during a great part of the year they are not even half so much; but at Kandy, a difference of 20° has sometimes been observed; and in the most elevated stations, the therm. has been known to vary nearly 50° between sunrise and mid-day. The annual average fall of 3 years at Kandy was 82 inches. Productions.] With some few exceptions, all the productions of which India and the Indian islands can boast are to be met with on this island, besides many others peculiar to itself. Dr. Hoffenmeister-an enthusiastic young German naturalist, whose career was prematurely closed in one of our late great battles on the Sutledge-thus describes his first impressions of the island. "A glimpse into the court-yard soon enticed us from our cool apartments into the open air. What a gorgeous show of red and yellow hibiscus! What a beautiful thick velvet turf, such as I had nowhere seen since I left England! Here grew the stately plumeria, with its exquisitely fragrant scent; there gigantic bananas, papay, and bread-fruit trees, overtopped the walls. We descended a flight of steps, twenty feet high, kept green by the perpetual warmth and moisture of the atmosphere, into the shrubbery, -a sort of wilderness, animated by countless living creatures. Through the tall grass, swarming with long-tailed green lizards, shone blue creeping plants of wondrous beauty (Clitoria), and vast quantities of red-blossomed balsams. There stood the bread-fruit tree (Artocarpus musa), with its jagged shining leaves, a foot broad, its white stem, and its enormous, yellowgreen, rough, bullet-shaped fruit; the graceful papay, with its regular, conical stem, bearing aloft its crown of blossoms, every leaf as broad as an umbrella, and with thick clusters of fruit underneath it; the fruit being like a small melon. Here, too, we found pesang trees (Musa paradisiaca), everywhere in India called bananas. The stem is reed-like, thick, and sappy; and the leaves, 8 feet long, shoot directly upwards. It seems scarcely conceivable that this luxuriant tree, 20 ft. high, and a foot in diameter, is but an annual. Under the dense shade of these enormous masses of foliage, there prevails a steaming atmosphere, exceedingly propitious for scorpions and serpents. A slender brown lizard, with a triangular head, slipped in and out between the branches; and a large kind of wood wasp (Xylocopa), filled the sultry air with its loud buzzing. Crows, with a much uglier croak than ours, sat on every tree, and looked down with impudent curiosity upon the intruders on their haunts. . . . Having passed through the street, we entered at once a thick grove of cocoa, and areca palms and bananas, that lines the whole coast. There is nothing to surpass in grace those tall, delicately-curved, slender palms, with their bushy crowns; how clumsy, CEYLON. illustrate the relative temp. in different parts of the island, the following table was prepared from observations recorded in the medical reports to the waroffice:- NUWERA-ELLIYA.

Minimum. in comparison with them, appears the African date, not to speak of any European tree! And what a marvellously-beautiful contrast they make with the deep blue sky, and the foaming surge, dashing high against the black rocks of the coast! Words can scarcely tell the singular impression made on the traveller, by the teeming fulness of tropical nature; the warm, moist air, heavy with the odours of spices, and cocoa-nut oil, and the fairy-like tone of the light, shining, broken, but clear, through the tufted palms. Thickets of yellow, red, and blue bell-flowers encompass the neat dwellings, built in the old Dutch style, with a verandah on the side, that border the road all the way to Colombo. Old Dutch inscriptions are met with everywhere, on half-decayed brick walls, overgrown with moss, as though in a region long forsaken by the living: everything makes on the mind an impression of dreamy quiet. Where the gardens are not filled with palms, the ground is hidden under a dense growth of shrubs, so much the lower as they are nearer to the sea. Countless small green snakes glide beneath the bushes; curiously-coloured crabs run about over the stones, and escape, with quick side-leaps, when pursued, under the tangled creepers of the beautiful red blossomed astragalus. The pine-apple and the pandang grow wild here, on the dry rocks, deriving their nutriment, to all appearance, only from the perpetual moisture of the air."-The tribe of the palms,-the most common, and at the same time the most magnificent and beautiful of eastern vegetation,—may be considered as the most generally useful to the Ceylonese. Among these the cocoa-nut tree unquestionably holds the first rank. This plant thrives well along the coast, and is thickly planted. It supplies the inhabitants with bread, milk, sugar, and oil; it affords them a strong spirit, vinegar, and yeast; its top is an excellant substitute for cabbage; it furnishes timber to build huts, and thatch to cover them; the shell of its nut is no mean article in the scanty catalogue of household utensils; and it supplies both cloth and cordage. The cultivation of this tree has, of late years, become as important as an article of commerce and export, as it has always been as an article of food and home-consumption to the inhabitants. During the last six years, between 4,000 and 5,000 acres have been planted by Europeans, and land suitable for this purpose is still in demand. The produce of these 4,000 acres may be estimated at 4,000 candies of copperahs—that is the fleshy part of the nut dried in the sun preparatory to crushingcapable of yielding 550 tons of oil, and this at a very moderate calculation. These new estates lie chiefly in the districts of Negombo, Jaffna, and Batticaloa; and when the addition thus made within so short a period is taken into consideration along with the prodigious extent of native plantations previously existing, which extend in a belt along the W coast | of the island almost from S to N, and on the E as low down as Batticaloa, an idea may be formed of the prodigious capabilities of C. in this one article now rising into so much demand in Europe. Large establishments for crushing the oil by steam have been opened at Colombo, where it is shipped to England, for the manufacture of candles by compression. By the recent improvements in cultivation introduced by the English speculators, the trees are now beginning to yield fruit in the fifth year, whereas native plantations seldom bear before the ninth or tenth. The export of coir-the fibre which lines the husk of the cocoa-nut-and its manufacture into ropes and cordage, have simultaneously increased; but the rudeness of the process, and the want of proper machinery, must always be a check on its improvement and extension. Coffee grows luxuriantly and with little care in the hilly country, and tobacco of a fine quality. In the Jaffna district, cotton, rice, and tobacco are extensively grown. Cinnamon, the bark of the Laurus cinnamomum, was until lately the chief export. Its growth is confined from about the middle of the W coast to a little beyond Dondra-head on the S, or between Colombo and Tangall. The production of this article has been greatly affected by the rivalship of Java, and by the introduction of cassia, though 30,000 individuals are still employed in its production over a space of some 2,000 acres.
