The coffee of C. is not of the best quality; but this is owing to the want of experience in the growerscertainly not to any defect in the soil or climate; and this may be remedied. The production of this article is rapidly, and even surprisingly, increasing. Thirty years ago, there was no export of coffee from C. In 1835, the export was only 1,870,143 lbs. In 1847 it had reached 33,000,000 lbs., a quantity nearly equal to the whole annual consumpt of Great Britain. —The soil is also well adapted for the growth of tobacco, which begins to attract great attention. In 1835 the gross quantity produced was nearly 6,000,000 lbs.; and in 1834 it was full 9,000,000.—The growth of rice is not encouraged as it ought to be, there not being enough for the native consumption, and large quantities are imported from the neighbouring continent. CEYLON. In 1847, indeed, C. exported rice to Great Britain. But this arose from the low freights from Colombo, as compared with those from Calcutta, to London,the former being from £5 10s. to £6 a-ton, while the latter was £8 to £8 10s.,—a difference which more than covered the charges of the transport of the rice from the Coromandel coast to C.—The tea-plant has lately been cultivated to some extent at Pusilava, at an alt. of 3,000 ft. above sea-level.—Sugar has been attempted to be cultivated on a considerable scale in C., but as yet with only problematical success. Between the year 1837, when the first estate was opened, and 1846, some 10 or 12 plantations had been made, and capital applied, varying in each case from £5,000 to £25,000; but even the most promising of these is still but experimentary, and most of the others have been abandoned. It seems probable that the most judicious means of treating the soils peculiar to this island have not yet been discovered. The estates attempted in the alluvial soils and near the banks of rivers have been hitherto the most promising, and next to them, those in the moist lands which had formerly been employed in the cultivation of rice. The plantations in elevated perhaps, remedy this defect: but the latter is difficult to procure, owing to the limited use of cattle in agriculture, and the great expense attendant on all artificial substitutes. [Parl. paper.]—European plants here degenerate rapidly. There is not to be found in the island one species identical with any European one. The sow-thistle, chickweed, mouse-ear chickweed, the corn spuny, and Poa annua grow here, but have been introduced. Forests extend over several provinces of the E and W divisions, and contain much useful and valuable timber. The ebony, calamander, and satin woods are most esteemed. State of agriculture, &c.] The most recent official reports from C. continue to state that, as an art, agriculture may be said to be unknown in any part of C., except amongst the Tamul pop. of Jaffna and the N. The Cingalese in the rest of the island raise their rice by irrigated fields, and their dry grain by burning down patches in the jungle; but in depart from the custom of their fathers. In Jaffna every cultivated spot is securely fenced; and as there are no hills and no rivers, the inhabitants would be helplessly dependent on the periodical rains, had they not dug one well or more in every field; and by incessant watering they succeed in obtaining a third harvest in each year, instead of two as formerly, one after each monsoon. For home-consumption they grow an innumerable variety of fruit and vegetables, almost unknown in the rest of the island-melons. gourds, brinjals, yams, cassava, country cabbage, and many others; and for trade and export, in addition to their great staple, tobacco, which is the finest in the E, they produce grain of all kinds. The plough and a large hoe are the principal agricultural instruments used. The plough is drawn by buffaloes, which are yoked by a simple wooden collar. The people of C. are more apathetic, indolent, and, in a word, barbarous, than any of the nations of the continent of India. The certain proof of this is, that although their island be under-peopled, and the wages of labour consequently comparatively high, they permit, without stirring a hand or foot, a great number of foreigners to settle, or sojourn among them. According to the statements of the recent committee on the slave-trade, not fewer than from 50,000 to 60,000 labourers of the Malabar or Tamul nation migrate yearly to C., and by their superior skill and thrift are enabled to carry back with them annually about £300,000 of savings. There is not a single native capitalist on the island; and no Cingalese is the owner of a vessel larger than a fishing - boat. Cotton is cultivated, but not in sufficient quantity to supply the consumption. In hemp-growing, some experiments have been made with success. In 1831, out of more than 2,000,000 of acres, only 300,000 were under crop, and 76,000 under pasturage. Animal kingdom.] C., if we except its ornithology, is less rich in the animal than in the vegetable creation; but it boasts of the largest and finest elephants in the world, great numbers of which are exported from the N districts. The number of these animals which infest the country, particularly the N mountains, and the depredations they commit on the plantations and rice-fields, have rendered it necessary from time to time to destroy the herds, by calling out and arming the inhabitants of a district to drive them into kraals or enclosures formed with the trunks of trees. Their yearly increment is supposed to be from 600 to 800. Latterly the government has kept an establishment of trained elephants for employment on the roads and public works. Certain classes of the inhabitants of the Kandyan provinces are required to catch and train elephants for the public services and for sale. Although the exportation of elephants was a source of profit to the Dutch government, the amount realized by government from the sale of elephants has not lately exceeded £61 per annum; and the amount produced by the sale of tusks, £57 per annum. Among the woods and jungles the ferocious buffalo exists; and the elk, stag, and deer abound.—Birds of the most splendid and beautiful plumage enliven the woods and thickets; amongst which are the peacock, the jungle-fowl, a great variety of the pheasant family, also parrots, pigeons, wood-peckers, and paddy-birds.—It need scarcely be added that all the noxious and disgusting classes of insects and reptiles are abundantly generated here amid the heat and moisture of the rich vegetable soil. Toads, scorpions, cockroaches, mosquitoes, centipedes of enormous size, wood-lice as big as walnuts, red, black, and white ants-the most numerous of the whole insect-tribe-infest every house: snakes, too, are not wanting, and these of the most poisonous kind. Alligators of a prodigious size infest the rivers; and the marshes abound in leeches. There is a land-leech, about three-quarters of an inch long, the Hirudo geometra of Linnæus, which abounds in the dry leaves and bushes, and is a terrible assailant. No sooner does a person stop where these leeches exist, than they crowd with great eagerness to the spot, from all quarters, and fasten upon him with the utmost avidity. They penetrate through stockings with * CEYLON. great ease, and reach the skin of the neck from the branches of trees. "During the night, they sometimes attack the face and gums of persons asleep. I have known eighty taken from one person at a time; and in some cases the blood trickles down under the clothes, so as even to appear coming over the edges of the shoes in walking. The pain occasioned by the bite is seldom acute; indeed, it commonly escapes notice, and the bloody clothes are the first indications of having been bitten. Much itching, which lasts for several days, follows the bites of leeches." [Marshall.]—The sea-fisheries are productive on all parts of the coast. Minerals. ] Minerals are numerous; iron is abundant in some provinces, and also manganese, chrome, and plumbago. In 1846, 25,036 cwt. of plumbago was exported, valued at £13,036. The very valuable mineral called tellur has also been found. Limestone is found near Kandy, and nitre and kaolin are abundant. Copper-ore has been discovered in the S part of the island, and quicksilver in the neighbourhood of Colombo.-Precious stones, especially the amethyst, the blue and yellow sapphire, the tourmaline, the garnet, ruby, and cat's eye, are frequently obtained, but not of a fine quality.-The great mass of the C. rock is, as we have said, of primitive formation, granite, or gneiss; the rocks along the shores are in general sandstone. Dolomite occurs in isolated positions in the interior. There are extensive salt formations on the coast; in some places it is formed spontaneously, in others by evaporation. Pearl fishery.] The pearl fishery, in the gulf of Manaar, is rather an object of precarious and hazardous speculation than of regular industry; and, in estimating the general resources of the island, is of inferior importance to the common fisheries. From the descriptions of ancient travellers, it appears to be still carried on nearly in the same manner that was practised several centuries ago; but as the trade was formerly open—the native princes receiving a tenth part of the pearls collected—there was more commercial activity than at present. The ancient towns of Mantotte and Putlam, and the districts around, probably derived much of their prosperity from the pearl-fisheries, which at all times have attracted a great concourse of speculators, and people in search of employment, from the continent. The fishing season used to create the utmost excitement along the whole coast of India, and as many as 4,000 or 5,000 people have congregated about the barren shores of Aripo,—which means 'a sieve,' and which appears to derive its name from the sifting and sorting the pearls through a succession of 10 or 12 brass cullenders of the size of a large saucer. The largest holes are in the one at the top, and the lower the pearl falls the less is its value. They are fished in the calm season, when land and sea breezes enable the boats to go out and return daily. The banks where these fisheries are carried on extend along the coast, from Manaar southward of Aripo, Condatchy, and Pomparippo. Some years ago fourteen beds existed, but they were not all productive, and only two or three of them were fished in one season. The principal bank is opposite Condatchy, and lies out at sea about 20 m.; it is about 10 m. in length, and 2 m. in breadth. The others are much smaller, and many of the banks are at present in a ruinous state, and may not become again productive for some years.—The pearloysters on these banks are all of one species, and of the same regular form; but they are of different qualities and denominations, according to the nature of the ground to which they are attached, and the appearance of the zoophytes which adhere to the outside of the shells. The shape of the oyster is an imperfect oval, pretty nearly the same as that of a cockle, and about 9½ inches in circumf. It is the Meleagrina Margaritifera, which inhabits the Persian gulf, the shores of C., the gulf of Mexico, the bay of Panama, and the gulf of California. According to the nomenclature of conchology, it is a bivalve, or is composed of two halves, and has at the posterior base a byssus or beard, for the accommodation of which there is a notch between the two shells. By this byssus and a glutinous matter which it secretes, the animal attaches itself to rocks, stones, dead shells, &c., and it is also perhaps an adjuvant to its motions. The size of the pearl-oyster varies in the different localities of its abode; but those of the same place do not differ much in this respect from each other: those of California, Panama, and Barhein in the Persian gulf, are large when compared with those taken from the pearl-banks of C., which measure at the hinge, which is linear, from 2 to 21 inches, and rectangularly to this base, from 21 to 3 inches. These shells are thin and diaphanous; internally nacred or pearlaceous, and externally rough, bearing the marks of the habitations of other animals, as sponges and some species of vermes, that penetrate the outside covering, or epidermis, and therein construct their dwellings. Those of the Persian gulf are thicker and of twice the above dimensions: they are smoother externally, covered with a greenish epidermis, and marked by dark-coloured rays of from a quarter to half-an-inch in breadth. Again, those found at Panama and those of California, particularly the latter, are very large, and the nacre is thick, forming what is termed 'mother-of-pearl.' A very considerable profit is derived from carrying these shells from California to China, where they are manufactured into a variety of ornamental and fancy articles. [Ruschenberger.] The outside of the shell is smooth, unless when covered with corals, sponges, and other marine productions; the inside is brighter and more beautiful than the pearl itself; the body of the animal is white, fleshy, and glutinous. The pearls are commonly contained in the thickest and most fleshy part of the oyster; they are composed of successive layers of a substance similar to the shell, and are supposed to be the effect of a disease to which the animal is subject. In general they are of a bright shining white; but some are occasionally found of a beautiful pink, others of the colour of gold, and a few of a jet black. An oyster commonly contains several pearls; and one has been known to produce 150, including the seed or dust pearls: but, on the contrary, an hundred oysters have been opened without yielding a single pearl which was discernible. Owing to certain disputes in which the Dutch were engaged, this fishery was neglected by them from 1768; but it was resumed by the British government in 1796, after they obtained possession of the coast. The produce of it has varied in different seasons. In 1797 the nett proceeds amounted to about £144,000, and in 1798 it produced a clear revenue of no less than £192,000; but the banks having been exhausted by the three preceding fisheries, it yielded only £30,000 the following season. The banks are fished on account of the government; the oysters are sold in lots of 1,000, on the spot, to the highest bidder. As there can be no certainty of the quantity or quality of pearls a heap of oysters may contain, the pearl-fishery must attract many to speculate, from the gamester-like interest thus thrown around it. In the month of November, between the close of the SW and commencement of the NE monsoon, when calms prevail, the banks are examined by the collector of Manaar, who is also the supervisor, attended by the inspector and an interpreter. The vessels employed on these examinations are a government guardvessel, two sailing-boats from the master-attendant's department at Colombo, and about eight native fishing-boats from Manaar and Jaffna. On these occasions the boats are furnished with one diving-stone and two divers. Five or six native headmen, called adapanaars, also attend and go in the boats to see that the divers perform their duty, and take notes of the reports given from time to time by the divers for the information of the supervisor. Some were of opinion that violent winds and currents buried the oysters in sand, or drove them entirely away; some supposed the adapanaars and divers employed at examinations gave false reports, and the banks were plundered by boats from the opposite coast. It was also said that former fisheries had been so extensive as to have injured the oyster-beds. The natives attributed it to various descriptions of fish, and also to a failure of seasonable rain, which they deem absolutely necessary to bring the oyster to perfection. To prevent plunder, a government vessel has been kept stationed on the banks during the season of the year that boats can visit them. To insure correct reports, diving-bells have been used to enable Europeans to go down at examinations. [Colombo Journal.] The fishing season commences in February; and, allowing for interruptions, commonly lasts about 30 days. During this time, all the boats regularly sail and return together with the land and the sea breeze. In each of the boats there are 10 divers, who go down into the sea by five at a time, and by diving alternately, have time to recruit in the interval. These people are accustomed to dive from their infancy. When one is about to descend, he seizes a rope, to which a large stone is attached, with the toes of his right foot, while he takes hold of a bag of net-work with those right hand, and holding his nostrils shut with his left, plunges into the water, and speedily reaches the bottom of the sea, where he collects as many oysters as he can, while he is able to remain under water, which is usually about two minutes. He then makes a signal to the rowers in the boat, by pulling the rope in his right hand, and is immediately drawn up, leaving the stone to be afterwards pulled up by the rope attached to it. The exertion of the divers in this process is so violent, that on getting into the boat they discharge water from their mouths, ears, and nostrils, and frequently even blood. But this does not prevent them from diving again in their turn. In the course of one day they often make from 40 to 50 plunges, and at each time bring up about a hundred oysters. Some rub their bodies over with oil, and stuff their ears and noses to prevent the water from entering them, while others use no precaution whatever. Although the usual time of remaining under water does not much exceed two minutes, yet there are instances of some who could remain four and even five minutes: and Mr. Beck informs us, in the Asiatic Researches, that he saw a Caffre boy remain no less than seven minutes. The pearls found at this fishery are of a whiter colour than those from the gulf of Ormus on the Arabian coast; but in other respects they are not accounted of so excellent a quality. The various kinds are sent to different markets. Those of the largest dimensions being most costly, and esteemed emblems of greatness, find a ready sale among the rich natives of the Nizam's dominions, the country of Guzerat, and other parts of India. Those of smaller size are chiefly in demand in England, France, Germany, and Russia, while the refuse find a ready market in China. Population.] The total pop. of the island is stated by Cordiner—on what authority we know not—at 1,500,000 souls; of whom the Cingalese, the Kandyans, and the Malabars, each constitute a third, or 500,000. "The first," he says, "occupy the coasts vinces, 399,408; for the interior or Kandyan provinces, 256,835; and for the N or Malabar districts, 195,697; making a total pop. of 851,940. Compared with the returns from the maritime provinces in 1814, the returns of 1825 exhibited an aggregate increase of 119,222 in 10 years, a rate at which the pop. would be doubled in 35 or 40 years. In 1835, the total pop. was returned at 1,241,825, of whom about 20,000 were foreigners and military. In 1843, the pop. was returned at 1,421,631, of whom 8,104 were whites. Taking the area at 25,000 sq. m., and the total pop. at 1,500,000, the density of the pop. will be 60 to the sq. m., or about one-fifth of that of Bengal.—The principal elements of the pop. of C. are the Kandyans, long the dominant race, who for three centuries after the conquest of the coast by Europeans preserved the independence of their mountain-capital and dynasty, and the Cingalese, properly so called, who inhabit the plains between the central highlands and the sea. These classes are as different as the Mamelukes and fellahs of Egypt, or the Arabs and dyaks of Borneo. Besides these two chief elements of pop., there is a considerable admixture of Arabs, of very ancient stock, called, like the Mahommedans of India, Moors; a large number of Malays; and a of his left. Having done this, he seizes another rope with his still greater proportion of settled or migratory Malabars. The central province of the island is the old seat of the Kandyans, which includes almost all the localities made memorable by our transactions. The Moors, who carry on most of the trade, are principally found in the E province; the Cingalese, as we have stated, round the greater part of the coast; and the Malabars in the N and W. We have the testimony of all writers on C., that the Cingalese are a mild race of men. Their stature is rather below the middle size; their limbs slender, but well-shaped, and in good proportion; their features more resemble the European than those of any other people of Asia; their colour is as various as the tints of bronze, but less deep on the whole than that of the Hindus; their eyes are dark; and their hairwhich they turn up and fix with a tortoise-shell comb on the top of the head-long, smooth, and jet black. The Kandyan women of rank are well-formed, middle-sized, and generally of a bamboo colour, with dark eyes, and a pleasing expression of feature. They walk well and unconstrained. They show not the least shyness before strangers, and appear on perfect equality with their lords. The dress of the chieftains is picturesque; and, when compared with the old paintings in their temples, appears not to have changed for ages. They wear a flat white turban; their beards are full and bushy; their hair is long, and tied up in like what we formerly called a club. A muslin or embroidered silk jacket, open in front, the sleeves fastened at the wrists with small gold buttons, tight to above the elbows, but puffed out to a ridiculous size at the shoulders, forms their upper dress; a painted calico or muslin cloth is worn round the loins, falling below the knee like loose trousers; while fold over fold of muslin round the waist gives them a pot-bellied appearance. In the folds of this cloth are carried their dagger, betel-box, and writing stylus. When walking, an attendant holds an ornamented talipot-leaf over them, which serves as an umbrella; two or four men march in front, carrying large silver-headed sticks, and a boy or page follows, bearing a sword. All men of distinction carry a long painted cane. The dress of the women of distinction is of fine cloth, with plain or coloured edges. It fits close to their bodies, and descends to their toes, with folds round the waist; they wear no veils or covering on their heads; but a handkerchief is thrown loosely over the left shoulder. Their hair is long, divided in the middle of the forehead, and tied up behind in a knot, like that of the Gentoo women. In the house they have no ornaments; but when abroad wear ear-rings, chains, and bracelets, generally of silver. Gold ornaments are usually confined to the wives of the adigars. They are cleanly in their habits, and careful of their persons, and keep their hair well-oiled. The legs of both men and women are always bare; nor is a sandal or slipper ever worn by them. The women have the management of the domestic affairs, and are said to be careful to stinginess. Yet it is remarkable that, although on apparent equality with their lords, the men always eat alone, and the mother and children in a separate apartment. They sit on a mat on the floor while eating. All ranks eat with their fingers off plantain leaves; people of distinction have china saucers to hold their numerous curries. Some animals and birds are avoided in food as unclean; but the flesh of the cow is that alone which their religion forbids them to eat. All ranks avoid touching the vessel with their lips while drinking. An earthen vessel, resembling our teapot, is in use for pouring liquid into the mouth. [Asiatic Journal.] The upper ranks can generally read and write their own language; and besides works on the life and doctrines of Budha, the Cingalese have many books, both | the performance of every description of service, the * CEYLON. in verse and prose, on moral subjects, grammar, medicine, astronomy, and various branches of literature common to other eastern nations, including a very complete history of the kings of C. They value literature, and oppressed as the cinnamon peelers are, Mr. Marshall testifies that many of them entertain a desire for information, and endeavour to give their children some degree of education. In 1814, of 2,000 of these peelers employed by the English government, 420 could read and write the Cingalese language. The houses of the Ceylonese are small and low, consisting only of one story. They are constructed of slender pieces of wood or bamboo, daubed over with clay, and covered with rice-straw or the leaves of the cocoa-tree. Round the walls of these huts are small banks of clay on which they sit or sleep. The benches, as well as the floors of their houses, they overlay with cow-dung, in order to preserve them from vermin, and to keep them smooth and clean. The furniture is of the most simple kind; and consists merely of a few instruments indispensably necessary for the preparation of their victuals. The native Ceylonese use neither tables, chairs, nor spoons; but, like other Indians, place themselves on the ground, and eat their food with their hands. The houses of the Kandyans are much superior to those of the Cingalese, whose villages, instead of presenting the regular compact appearance of a town, look like a number of miserable huts scattered through the midst of a thick forest. There is not the smallest regularity observable among them; but every individual places his cottage near the centre of a cocoa-tree tope, in the most convenient spot he can find. In mountainous parts of the country, where the natives live in continual danger of attack from wild beasts, or suddenly overtaken by inundations, it is usual for them to build their huts on the summit of rocks, or even on the top of lofty trees. Some of them also fix a number of high posts in the ground, and place on these a kind of hurdle which serves them for a nocturnal habitation. The language and customs of the Kandyans, who occupy the interior and central division, are in some respects peculiar; and have undergone but little change from a very remote period. The civil institutions of such of them as profess the religion of Bhud, are obviously of Hindu origin. The possession of land on tenures of service, and the division of the people into classes according to their trades or occupations, subsist also among the Malabars, and probably prevailed throughout the continent of India before the Mahommedan conquests; but the abolition of the religious distinctions of castes has constituted a marked peculiarity in the institutions of the Bhuddists, which drew on them the hostility of the Brahmins, and produced those religious wars which depopulated the country, and led to the settlemen; of the Malabars in the N district of the island. The civil distinctions of caste have doubtless originated in an attempt to introduce a division of labour; and these distinctions have been rendered hereditary from the privileges acquired by particular castes, and by the practice of assigning lands for the conditional performance of labour or service. Although the ostensible employment of particular castes has frequently changed, and different occupations are constantly engaged in by people of the same caste, yet the intermarriage of the high and low castes is prohibited by Kandyan law, and many absurd distinctions are recognised and enforced by which the lower castes are reduced to an almost servile condition, which has become hereditary. The highest and most esteemed caste is that of Vellales or Goyas, whose occupations are purely agricultural; but as land is assigned for practice of agriculture is not confined to this class. The lands are generally cultivated by small hereditary proprietors or tenants, and usually under a conditional tenure, obliging them to make contributions, or to perform some public or private service; although in some instances the lands are held under free grants. "The difficulties periodically experienced in the administration of C. have always primarily arisen from the surviving institutions of the old central kingdom of Kandy, which, though it barely comprised a fourth of the island in actual extent, was enabled by the ancient prestige of its rule to influence the adjoining provinces, and by the strength of its position to defy for a long period the arms of the colonists. The Kandyan race exclusively supplied the royal family, the aristocracy, and the priesthood, who, jointly and severally, exercised the most absolute dominion over the rest of the people. At our final conquest of the kingdom in 1815, the allegiance of the chiefs and people was, by free and formal convention, transferred to the British crown; and a nominal emancipation of the people followed,—that is to say, serfdom was abolished, and the absolute sovereignty of the Kandyan crown in and over the lands of the kingdom was commuted for certain easy payments. By the same convention it was stipulated that the religion of the country-Buddhism-should be kept inviolate, and that no injury should be done to the temples or priests. These conditions, however, were but imperfectly fulfilled. The small number of British functionaries resident in the island rendered it necessary to employ native agency very largely in the local administration, and for this purpose a system was established which practically resulted in the maintenance of the chiefs in all their ancient privileges. A single Englishman was the nominal superintendent of an immense district, but he relied for all information and aid upon the old feudal lords, or 'headmen' of the province, who, in their turn, were served by petty headmen, through whom alone any communication with the people could be held. The consequence of all this was a very false and perilous position of the English government. We were depressing the chiefs with one hand and exalting them with the other. By our legislation we were promoting equality and freedom; by our administration we were subjecting the people more than ever to the influences of feudalism, and were thus placing almost unlimited power in the hands of those very parties whom by our political ordinances we were exasperating and estranging." [Times.] There is a race known among the Kandyans by the name of Bedas or Vedahs, who live in a free and independent state in the inaccessible mountains and forests of Bintan, behind Batticollo. They seek their food in the deep forests abounding with elephants, buffaloes, wild hogs, elks, and antelopes; and cautiously abstain from all connection with the rest of the islanders, except in bartering with the borders on their forests, ivory, deer-skins, dried flesh, and honey, for salt, arrows, cloth, and a few other articles. They are a robust and hardy race, courageous and resolute, but treacherous. Their language is a dialect of Cingalese; and the faint notion which they have of religion approaches nearer to Brahmanism than to Buddhism. The next class of inhabitants—who were reckoned to form one-half of the pop. of the British possessions before the addition of the Kandyan dominions—are the Malabars. They occupy, as we have said, the northern districts; and are the same active, enterprising, crafty people, in their character of merchants, pedlers, jewellers, workers-in-metals, tailors, fishermen, jugglers, that we find them on the continent whence they originally came. About one-half of these people are indifferent Mahommedans; the other half, worse Hindus. The Malays, who are found on almost every island in the Indian seas, are here pretty numerous; they are soldiers, sailors, fishermen, and artificers. Many of them were introduced by the Dutch in a state of slavery. Among the various nations who inhabit C., the Malays are the only people of whom we have been able to make soldiers. Many thousands of Africans (Caffres) have been introduced into the island, both as slaves and as soldiers, by the successive European powers who have had possession of the maritime territory; but they never kept up their numbers, and have nearly disappeared. The number of Dutch in the island does not exceed 900. With the exception of a few families, they have been reduced to comparative indigence by our capture of the island.—Of the Portuguese who first opened the way to India, few traces now remain in C., but the ruins of their former grandeur. Their name, language, religion, and religious establishments, still exist; but the Portuguese themselves have disappeared. Religion.] The language and religion of the Kandyans are the same as those of the Siamese, from whom they consider themselves descended. The religion of Brahma is said to have prevailed in Ceylon till the 6th cent. B. C., when that of Budha obtained the ascendency. Many of the towns and villages yet retain the name of Hindu deities; and the ruins of their temples yet surround modern edifices of worship constructed to Budha. In 1811, the number of temples dedicated to Budha, and other inferior deities of Cingalese superstition, amounted to 1,200. In 1848, an ordinance imposing on every male inhabitant of C., from 18 to 55, the burden of six days' labour on the public roads, commutable into a payment of 3s. was promulgated by the governor. Within a few months after the appearance of this ordinance, a petition was presented to the governor, from 550 Buddhist priests, claiming exemption from its operation. In this document the petitioners state that the Buddhist religion was established in C. 316 years before the birth of Christ; that the native governments, the Portuguese, the Dutch, and the British governments, down to the time of Lord Torrington, never compelled them to work; that by the treaty of 1815, the Buddhist religion is "inviolable," its rites, ministers, and places of worship to be protected. They add that should they now be "compelled to work," they would "not only infringe the rites of their religion, but violate the same completely, and their prospects in a world to come be blighted, for which they have been labouring all their life." It appears that the priests of Buddha in C., in Ava, Pagu, Siam, Canboja, Cochin-China, and even China, are, by their vows, forbidden to labour, or to possess money, or any other kind of property. They must live on charity; they are not allowed to stitch or wash their own garments, or sweep their own dwellings, or even cook their own food, which, in alms, is always given to them ready dressed. They are not the owners of the temples in which they perform worship, nor of the monasteries in which they dwell, nor of the lands annexed to them for their maintenance. Among the various Christian denominations throughout the island, the Roman Catholics greatly preponderate; and the governor reports that multitudes who call themselves Christians in public, are in secret closely attached to Buddhism and the Hindu mythology! The church of England has a bishop and several clergymen on the island; and the church of Scotland, Wesleyan Methodists, Baptists, and American mission are likewise engaged in the work of religious and intellectual teaching in C. The exertions of the American missionaries have received special commendation. Through their efforts chiefly a Tamul college has been established at Batticotta, in which, since its opening in 1823 down to 1846, 570 students had been educated. A large printing establishment too has been actively employed in connexion with this mission since 1833, which issues upwards of 50,000 volumes a-year, chiefly of an educational and elementary character. The total extent of roads in 1841 was 1,572 m.; in 1846, 2,358 m. A line of road from Manaar on the NW coast to the great central line, 45 m. in length, is now in process of execution. The governor reports, in 1846, that "there is no one district in the island whose prosperity is not at present retarded, by the insufficiency of means of transit and traffic. In the N prov., the vast and fertile provinces of the Wanny, once a separate kingdom, and the productive plains of Nuwera-Calawa, formerly the granary of the C. sovereigns, are now deserted solitudes, owing to the utter want of roads of any description, with the exception of one line from N to S, the instantaneous effects of which on the cultivation and prosperity of the sections of country in immediate contact with it were almost incredible; rice and other produce, before unsaleable, having at once become double in value by the demand created through this new outlet. Diverging lines in other directions, but connected with this main trunk, will be productive of incalculable advantages to these districts; and on them must depend the success of any attempt that may be hereafter made to repair the stupendous tanks and channels for irrigation, by which these provinces were once rendered the most luxuriant in the island; as, owing to their decay, they are now the most forgotten and unproductive. The E prov. has manar, whence they are passed over to Ramisseram. By these means, letters from Colombo may reach Madras in 8 or 10 days, and Calcutta and Bombay in from 17 to 20 days. Letters conveyed by steamers from Point-de-Galle, reach Madras in 3 days, and Calcutta in 9. The communication between C. and the straits of Malacca, China, Java, and Manila, is carried on monthly by means of steamers from Point-de-Galle. Letters from Australia, by way of Calcutta, occupy about 3 months in the transit. Government. At a convention held on the 2d March, 1815, in the Cingalese year 1736, at the palace in the city of Kandy, between Lieut. - Gen. Brownrigg and the adigaars, dissaves, and other principal Kandyan chiefs, the reigning raja, of Malabar race, was solemnly deposed; all claim or title of the Malabar race to the Kandyan throne was declared to be extinguished; and the dominion of the Kandyan provinces to be vested in the sovereign of the British empire, and to be exercised through the governors of C. for the time being, and their agents, saving to all chief and subordiate native headmen the rights and privileges of their respective offices, and declaring the religion of Buddha to be "inviolable," and that its rites, ministers, and places of worship, are to be maintained and protected.—Several charters have from time to time been issued for the administration of justice in C. Under these charters, there were a supreme court of judicature, and a high court of appeal; but the latter was abolished in 1833. The supreme court is composed of the chief-justice and two puisne judges; and forms a court of appellate urisdiction for the district courts. The governor has the power to respite the sentence of the supreme court in the case of persons condemned to death; and in civil cases above £500 appeal lies to the queen in council. The judges are aided by a respectable bar. Courts of request, police magistrates, justices of the peace, and coroners, have been appointed in various districts. The judicial division of the island comprises three circuits.—The law prevailing in the maritime provinces is the Roman-Dutch law called 'the Statutes of Batavia.' Trial by jury was introduced in 1811; and every free native of C. of 21 years of age, is qualified to be a juryman. Revenue, &c.] The public revenue of C. may be divided into two branches: viz., one derived from certain productions of the island reserved by government for the fiscal resources; the other, such imposts as the land-tax, taxes on property, taxes on consumption, and capitation-taxes. The gross aggre- CEYLON. gate revenue of C. may be stated at somewhat more than £450,000 per annum; but great expenses attend the collection of some of the principal branches of it. The following table exhibits the revenue and expenditure of C. from 1821 to 1847: Revenue. Expenditure. Years. Surplus. Deficiency. £ £ £ ... The export duty payable on cinnamon-till recently the great staple produce of C.—before its recent reduction from 1s. to 4d. per lb., amounted to about 75 per cent. of its value. Rice is the staple food of all classes, the sole food of the poorer classes, yet every native who puts a plough into the ground to grow rice is taxed; and on every bushel of rice imported from the coast of India—where it costs from 2s. to 2s. 6d. a bushel—a tax of 7d. is levied on landing. The 'paddy tax' varied originally in different localities, and in different circumstances, from one-half to one-fourth, one-fifth, one-tenth, or one-fourteenth of the produce. Till 1826 it was levied by an annual assessment of the crop of each harvest; since that year the assessment has, in most districts, been commuted for an annual payment of a given quantity of grain. This change was only gradually introduced, and is not yet universally adopted. The revenue of C. for 1845 was £454,000, in round numbers, which implies a taxation at the rate of 6s. a-head of the pop. The revenue of the Madras territory is at the rate of 4s. 2d. a-head only; so that by this test, the people of C. were taxed 44 per cent. higher than those of Madras. But the people of the Bengal provinces pay only 3s. a-head; so that the Cingalese were more highly taxed than the Bengalese, by above 70 per cent. The removal of nearly all duties on exports reduced the estimate of revenue for 1849 to £408,300, which reduces the taxation per head to about 5s. 5d., or no more than 30 per cent. beyond the rate of taxation in the Madras territory, or 54 beyond that of Bengal. From the abolition of export duties, however, no direct benefits whatever accrued to the Cingalese, because these were never paid by them; but, of course, always by the stranger. They continue, in fact, now to pay, as heretofore, all the direct taxes, and all taxes on consumption. [Daily News. The heads of expenditure for 1846 were as follows:-Governor and civil establishment, £197,090. Judicial and police, £53,310. Survey and roads, £24,958. Public works, £44,949. Military and military works, £75,645. Ecclesiastical establishment, £7,418. Education, £8,182. Pensions, £25,191. Charities, £3,806. Miscellaneous, £8,671. Arrears of former years, £48,985. Total, £498,205.—The heads of revenue for 1846 were as follows:-Monopolies, including the distillation and sale of arrack, wines, spirits, and the sale of salt, £96,857. Taxes on the cultivation of corn, £44,004. Import and export duties, £140,279. Regular revenue, including tolls, stamps, postage, &c., £60,765. Casual revenue, £55,790. Arrears, £18,698. Total, £416,403. There is a considerable amount of government paper-money current in C. in the form of notes payable in specie, on demand, at the Treasury, in sums of not less than £1 sterling. This paper-money was introduced to take the place of the old rix-dollar notes, which, however, did not entirely disappear from circulation until 1832. The rix-dollar notes were introduced in 1801 as a substitute for copper money, at that time the only circulating medium. The colony was actually in debt when we took it from the Dutch, and the British government adopted that debt so far as to take upon it to pay the interest; the paper rix - dollars formed a tempting resource to meet the exigencies of government; and as year after year the expenses of C. exceeded its receipts, each successive governor increased the issue of paper-money. This state of things has been put an end to; and the present amount of outstanding government notes—not exceeding £87,000—is the only remaining portion of the unpaid debt of the island .- The Bank of C., established in 1841 by royal charter, possesses a nominally paid-up capital of £125,000. The Oriental bank, opened in 1843, is a branch of the Oriental bank of India. Weights and Measures.] The imperial standard is adopted in C. by government and Europeans; but the Dutch, and the native standards are frequently used by other classes. The Dutch parah, a cube of 11.57 inches, is divided into 24 seers. The weight of the parah for coffee is from 35 to 50 lbs.; for pepper, 27 to 30 lbs.; salt, 52 to 55 lbs.; rice, 42 to 46 lbs., in the husk, 30 to 33 lbs. In native dry measure, 4 chundus make 1 measure or seer; 4'8 seers, 1 coorney; 21 coorneys, 1 markal; 2 markals, 1 parah; 8 parahs, 1 ammonam; 93 ammonams, 1 last. The smallest measure of quantity, dry or liquid, is the hundua or handful.—The bale of cinnamon consists of about 921 lbs.—In Kandyan square measure of surface, 8 lahas make 1 coornie, which is equal to 10.9375 sq. perches; 10 coornies make 1 peyla; 4 peylas, 1 ammonam = 2 acres, 2 sq. roods, 37½ sq. inches.—The native measures of space are singular. In estimating distances, the whoo or loud hollo is the shortest measure; <math>2 \text{ whoos} = 1 \text{ attakmé}</math>; <math>4 \text{ attakmés} = 1 \text{ gow}</math>; 5 gows are reckoned a day's journey, or from 25 to 30 m.—Cingalese coins are rare. Every sort of English money is now in use, besides rix-dollars of English coinage, value 1s. 6d. each; Spanish and American dollars, value 4s. 2d. each; and Company's rupees, value 2s. each. Topography.] Jaffnapatam, built on a tongue of land in N lat. 9° 36', and E long. 79° 50', is a great resort of the Dutch families: the province itself comprehends rather less than one-fourth of the whole island, and has several small islands attached to it. For a part of the distance between Jaffnapatam and Manaar, it is not very easy to say whether the country is sea or land: the surface is water; but it is in general so shallow, and the bottom under it so firm, that it can be walked over.—Still farther S, on the coast opposite to Coromandel, there is a singular peninsula, Calpentyn, which lies parallel to the shore, from which it is divided by a narrow slip of water, which extends between 60 and 70 m. This peninsula, though sandy, is thickly covered with cocoa-nut trees and palmyra palms; and the country on the other side of the bay, or rather natural ditch, is very rich and beautiful.—After passing Chilaw southward, the mountains begin to make their appearance, while the coast retains its beauty, and indeed gets more luxuriant in appearance as Colombo is approached. At Negombo, about 40 m. to the N of Colombo, the cinnamon-country begins; and it extends to a considerable distance S of that city.—Colombo is a very beautiful place, and with proper attention to avoiding the direct action of the sun, very healthy. The city itself is nearly insulated by water, but the country immediately across the lake is the most rich and pic- CEYLON. turesque that can be imagined. Small vessels only can approach the shore at Colombo, and while the SW monsoon blows, ships cannot ride in the roadstead, but must either lie at Calpentyn, an accessible port in the gulf of Manaar, or pass round to Trincomalee. There is a canal communication from Colombo to Calpentyn; and another has been opened into the Caltura river, 25 m. to the S of Colombo. A carriage-road has been executed from Colombo to Kandy.—S from Colombo the cocoa-nut trees get still more plentiful; and the formation of cables of the fibres is one of the staple manufactures of some of the villages on the coast. This richness and beauty continue along the whole of the SW and S; and there is a tolerable harbour at Point-de-Galle, a few miles to the W of the southmost point of the island. The lighthouse recently erected here is in N lat. 6° 1', W long. 80° 14'.—The province of Matura, in the extreme S, is celebrated for its scenery: groves and thickets here alternating much more with open glades than in other parts of the island.—Dondra-head, the southmost part of the island, lies a few miles to the E of the little town of Matura.—After passing the S point, the character of the coast changes much for the worse: it is in this quarter unhealthy, covered with wood, broken by salt marshes, and infested with wild beasts. This general character of coast continues all the way to Trincomalee; thence to Jaffnapatam it partakes of the character of the coast immediately to the S of that settlement.—The town of Mantotte, now in ruins, is said to have been the capital of a kingdom founded by the Brahmins. Contiguous to Mantotte is an immense reservoir, called the Giant's tank, 16 or 18 m. in extent. At the distance of about 9 m. from this tank, an embankment, constructed of immense stones cemented with lime, has been laid across the Moesely or Aripo river, in order to collect the water, and lead it by means of canals into the tank. The length of this dam is 600 ft.; its breadth in some parts 60, in none less than 40 ft.; and its height from 8 to 12 ft. These works indicate the ancient existence of some powerful and populous nation in the island,—an opinion which is farther confirmed by the astonishing works to be met with in the district of Tangalle, and around the lake of Candelay, distant about 16 m. from Trincomalee. Nor can we omit to mention a singular monument discovered by Mr. Sowers, collector of Batticaloa, in the year 1810, in the centre of a thick forest. It is supposed to have been a Buddha pagoda, reared, like the Egyptian pyramids, in honour of the dead. The size of the building is gigantic; the basis of its cone is about 4 m. in circumf., and on the tops and sides large trees have fixed their roots among the ruins, up to the height of 50 or 60 ft. It is surrounded by a square enclosure, 1 m. in circumf., consisting of a broad wall, made of brick and mortar, and having within it a number of cells. - The city of Kandy is situated in the central province of Tallanour, surrounded on all sides by lofty mountains. It is about 2 m. in length, and consists of a number of mud-built houses, surrounded by a mud-wall. The only buildings of any consequence in Kandy are the temples of Buddha, and the royal palace, which is a square edifice of immense dimensions.—Ramiseram, the holy island of Rama, is situate at the N extremity of Ceylon, about 20 m. from the shore. It is a low flat island, 20 m. in circumf.; and may be considered as the most southerly pier of that series of shoals and coral rocks which—as already noticed -under the name of Rama's or Adam's bridge, serves to connect C. with the coast of Coromandel. Historical notice.] C. was a great emporium of trade in Roman times, yet little is known of its history previous to the arrival of the Portuguese under Almeida, in 1505. The natives, long harassed by the attacks of the Arabs, readily consented to pay a tribute in cinnamon to these Europeans, in consideration of being assisted against their fierce invaders. Not content with a friendly alliance, however, the Portuguese endeavoured to form a settlement on the island, in which, after a long and severe struggle, they succeeded: the sea-coast fell into their hands, but the interior remained in the possession of its original inhabitants. After the lapse of a century, the Dutch found their way to the East, and reached C. in 1603. They were favourably received by the oppressed natives; and, after a sanguinary struggle of nearly fifty years, they at last compelled the Portuguese to yield the island to them in 1656. The conduct of the new settlers soon proved as offensive to the natives as that of their predecessors; and hostilities of long duration took place in which the Ceylonese were eventually driven to the mountains and jungles of the interior. In 1766 a treaty was at last concluded between the two parties, which left to the native king of Kandy the name and somewhat of the appearance of royalty. The progress of the American war brought the British to this island; but their arms, though victorious, were not rewarded with permanent success till the contest with France and her allies induced our government to despatch a powerful expedition against C., which took possession of the Dutch territories in this island in 1796. A treaty of alliance and commerce with the Kandyans was projected in 1800, but failed. Our troops took possession of their capital in 1803; but, unable to maintain their conquest, were forced to capitulate. The terms of surrender, however, were grossly violated, and the detachment was put to the sword under circumstances of savage cruelty. In 1815, an expedition of 3,000 men, under the command of General Brownrigg, in concert with the native population, who had become weary of their tyrannical and bloodthirsty monarch, Raja Sri Wikrama Raja Singha, entered Kandy in triumph, and deprived the native monarch of all power. The deposed monarch was finally sent to Nellore, where he died in 1832. In 1817, a harassing rebellion broke out in the central provinces of C., which lasted until the end of 1819: but since that period, until very recently, uninterrupted peace has prevailed, and various improvements, fiscal, judicial, and commercial, have been carried into effect. The Kandyan provinces are separately administered by the governor, without the assistance of his council; and the whole island may now be regarded as a British colony under the Crown, and having its ports and commerce open to the whole British people. There is no doubt that the possession of C. was turned to good account by the Portuguese and Dutch, although until lately a vote of supply was annually made for the support of our Ceylonese establishment. The resources of this valuable island have not yet been fully opened up; but under the liberal system of colonization now pursuing, it may be anticipated that the value of this dependency will become yearly more apparent. It is not in a commercial view alone that we are to estimate the value of this possession, which is one, says M. Bartolacci, that "in the event of a great reverse of fortune on the continent of India, would still afford us a most commanding position, invulnerable by the Indian powers in the peninsula, and yet so situated as to give us the greatest facility of regaining the sovereignty of that country."-" The harbour of Trincomalee is open to the largest fleets in every season of the year, when the storms of the SW and NE monsoons render impracticable, or very dangerous, the approach to other parts in India. This circumstance alone ought to fix our attention to that spot, as peculiarly adapted to be made a strong military depot, and a place of great mercantile resort, if a generally free trade becomes effectually established from India to other parts of the world. It ought farther to be observed, that the narrowness of the channel which separates the island of C. from the continent of India, and the position of Adam's Bridge, which checks the violence of the monsoons, leaves on either side of it a calm sea, and facilitates a passage to the opposite coast at all times of the year. A respectable European force stationed at Colombo, Jaffnapatam, or Trincomalee, can, in a very few days, or hours, be landed on the Malabar and Coromandel provinces." The possession of such a station as this, among the rich islands of the Indian archipelago, is of the utmost importance to a commercial nation.—The earliest account, in our language, of this island, is an exceedingly amusing and instructive narrative written by Robert Knox, who, in 1659, was kidnapped by the king of Kandy, and detained nineteen years in his dominions. The narratives of Percival and Cordiner, both published since the commencement of the present century, will be found to contain a considerable amount of correct and interesting information regarding C. Much valuable information has also been furnished by the reports of our Bible and Missionary societies. One of the most valuable works that has yet appeared on Ceylon is Bartolacci's view of its agricultural, commercial, and financial interests, published in 1817, and accompanied by a large and comprehensive map from the latest surveys. But the authorities chiefly followed in this article are the Official reports of Lieutenant-colonel Colebrooke, drawn up in 1831-2; the annual Reports transmitted to the Colonial office; and Mr. Pridham's recent work, entitled, 'An Historical, Political, and Statistical Account of Ceylon and its Dependencies.' Lond. 1849. 2 vols. 8vo.

CEYRESTE, or CERESTE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhône, cant. of La Ciotat, 16 m. ESE of Marseilles, on a CHA hill. Pop. 700. It is surrounded by ancient ramparts, and contains a square, one side of which is lined with handsome houses. The streets are generally narrow. This village occupies the site of the ancient Citharista or Cæsarista; and in the vicinity are the ruins of a castle supposed to have been built by Cæsar. The environs are extremely fertile, and produce fruit in great abundance and wine.

CEYZERIAT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. of Bourg. The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,576; in 1841, 8,331. It produces wine in large quantities. -The town is 6 m. ESE of Bourg, in a pleasant and salubrious situation. Pop. 981. In the vicinity is a ferruginous spring, called, from the colour of its waters, Fontaine-Rouge.
CEYZERIEU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Virieux-le-Grand, 6 m. from Belley. Pop. 1,831. It contains silk spinning-mills. The locality is very fertile.
CE'ZAC, or CEYSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Saint-Savin, 15 m. E of Blaye. Pop. 1,709.
CE'ZAINE (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Burie. Pop. 1,022.—Also a commune in the dep. of Var, cant. of Saint-Vallier. Pop. 1,216.
CEZAN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Gers, cant. and 9 m. WSW of Fleurance, and 11 m. SW of Lectoure. Pop. 588.
CEZANNE. See CESANNA.

CEZE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the Cevennes, in the dep. of Lozere, 25 m. ESE of Mende; crosses the N part of the dep. of Gard, passing Saint-Ambroix and Bagnols; and after a total course, in a generally ESE direction, of about 60 m. falls into the Rhone, on the r. bank, 6 m. NW of Roquemaure. Gold in small quantities has been occasionally found in its channel.
